---
name: ddx
description: Operates the DDx toolkit for document-driven development. Covers beads (work items), the queue, executions, agents, harnesses, personas, reviews, spec-id. Use when the user says "do work", "drain the queue", "run the next bead", "execute a bead", "review this", "check against spec", "what's on the queue", "what's ready", "create a bead", "file this as work", "run an agent", "dispatch", "use a persona", "how am I doing", "ddx doctor", or mentions any ddx CLI command.
---

# DDx

DDx (Document-Driven Development eXperience) is a CLI platform for
document-driven development. It ships a bead tracker (portable work
items with acceptance criteria), an agent service boundary (DDx
orchestrates work while the upstream agent routes providers/models), a persona system (bindable AI
personalities), a library registry (plugins with prompts, templates,
personas), and git-aware synchronization. This skill makes any
skills-compatible coding agent (Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, Gemini
CLI, etc.) understand and operate the DDx surface correctly.

## How this skill works

The skill body you're reading is an **overview** plus an **intent
router**. The real domain guidance lives in `reference/*.md` files.

**Directive: before responding to any DDx-related request, read the
matching reference file from the router table below. The router is
not optional — your answer must be grounded in the reference file's
guidance, not this overview alone.**

## Vocabulary

Single source of truth for DDx terms. Every reference file uses these
exact definitions.

- **Bead** — a portable work item (task, bug, epic, chore) with
  metadata, dependencies, and acceptance criteria. `ddx bead create`.
- **Queue** — the set of open beads. The *ready queue* is the subset
  with all dependencies closed. `ddx bead ready`, `ddx bead blocked`.
- **Ready** — a bead whose dependencies are all closed and which is
  eligible to be picked up next. `ddx bead ready`.
- **Blocked** — a bead with at least one unclosed dependency.
  `ddx bead blocked`.
- **Claim** — mark a bead as in-progress by an agent (concurrent-write
  protection). `ddx bead update <id> --claim`.
- **Close** — mark a bead as done, with evidence (session, commit
  SHA). `ddx bead close <id>`. Beads only close on execution outcomes
  `success` or `already_satisfied`.
- **Run** — one agent invocation atom. `ddx run` calls upstream agent
  `Execute` once with prompt/config, `MinPower`/`MaxPower` bounds, and optional
  passthrough constraints.
- **Try** — one bead attempt in an isolated worktree. `ddx try <id>` wraps
  `ddx run` with bead prompt resolution, evidence capture, and merge/preserve
  finalization.
- **Work** — drain the bead queue. `ddx work` picks ready beads and invokes
  `ddx try`; it owns queue iteration and retry policy.
- **Execution** — a generic DDx execution run (FEAT-010). Includes execution
  definitions, execution records, and execution evidence under `.ddx/runs/<id>/`.
- **Agent** — an AI coding agent (Claude, Codex, Gemini, etc.)
  invoked via a harness. Not a subagent (harness-specific — see
  below).
- **Harness** — an upstream agent routing concept. DDx may pass `--harness` as
  an operator-supplied constraint, but DDx does not validate, rank, fallback, or
  branch on harness names.
- **Persona** — a Markdown file (YAML frontmatter + body) that
  defines an AI personality. DDx injects the body as a system-prompt
  addendum to `ddx run`. `ddx persona list/show/bind`.
- **Role** — an abstract function (e.g., `code-reviewer`,
  `test-engineer`) a workflow can reference. Projects bind roles to
  personas.
- **Binding** — a project-specific `role: persona` map in
  `.ddx/config.yaml` under `persona_bindings`.
- **Power bounds** — `MinPower` and optional `MaxPower` integers passed to the
  upstream agent. DDx may raise `MinPower` on eligible retries; the agent owns
  the concrete harness/provider/model routing.
- **Plugin** — a self-contained extension installed to
  `.ddx/plugins/<name>/`. The default `ddx` plugin (personas,
  prompts, patterns, templates) is auto-installed by `ddx init`.
  `ddx install <name>`.
- **Skill** — an agentskills.io-standard directory (SKILL.md +
  optional `reference/`, `evals/`, `scripts/`). This `ddx` skill is
  the one DDx ships. Plugins can ship additional skills.
- **Subagent** — a harness-local concept for running a prompt in an
  isolated context (Claude Code's `.claude/agents/` + `context:
  fork`; Codex's `agents/`; others differ). DDx does not specify subagent
  orchestration; that remains harness business.
- **Update** — refresh plugin/toolkit content to a newer version.
  `ddx update [<plugin>]`.
- **Upgrade** — replace the DDx binary with a newer release.
  `ddx upgrade`.
- **Review** — two distinct concepts. **Bead review**
  (`ddx bead review <id>`) grades a completed bead against its
  acceptance criteria. **Comparison/adversarial review** is a workflow skill
  composition over `ddx run`, not a core quorum flag. See `reference/review.md`.
- **Governing artifact** — the document that authorizes a bead's
  work: a FEAT-\*, SD-\*, TD-\*, or ADR-\* under `docs/`. Referenced
  via `spec-id`.
- **Spec-id** — the `spec-id: <ID>` custom field on a bead pointing
  at its governing artifact.

## Intent router

Before responding, read the matching file.

| User says / asks about | Read this file |
|---|---|
| write/plan work, "create a bead", "file this as work", bead metadata, acceptance criteria, dependencies | `reference/beads.md` |
| "do work", "drain the queue", "run the next bead", "execute a bead", "run work", verify-and-close | `reference/work.md` |
| "review this", "check against spec", bead review, quorum review, code review, adversarial check | `reference/review.md` |
| "score a bead", "triage a failed attempt", "refine a bead", bead authoring lint | `bead-lifecycle/` |
| "run an agent", "dispatch", harness/provider/model passthrough, power, effort, "use a persona", role bindings | `reference/agents.md` |
| "what's on the queue", "what's ready", "how am I doing", health check, "ddx doctor", sync status | `reference/status.md` |

If the intent spans multiple files (e.g., "create a bead and then
run it"), read beads.md first, then work.md. If no match, ask the
user which concept they mean rather than guessing.

## Top-level policy reminders

These apply across all DDx operations. Do not restate them in every
reference file; do not violate them.

- **Never edit `.ddx/beads.jsonl` directly.** All tracker changes go
  through `ddx bead create/update/close/dep`. Direct edits corrupt
  bead history and cannot be audited.
- **Tracker changes are commit-worthy.** After `ddx bead create`,
  `update`, `dep add/remove`, or `close`, commit the resulting
  `.ddx/beads.jsonl` change — either as a tracker-only commit or
  folded into the same commit as related implementation changes.
- **Preserve bead-attempt commit history.** Branches containing
  `ddx try` / `ddx work` execution commits carry an audit trail. **Never
  squash, rebase, filter, or amend** these commits. Use only
  `git merge --ff-only` or `git merge --no-ff` when merging.
  `gh pr merge --squash` and `--rebase` are forbidden on these
  branches.
- **Work in worktrees for parallel agents.** Use `wt switch -c
  <branch>` (worktrunk) or equivalent to give each concurrent agent
  its own isolated checkout. Execute-bead does this automatically;
  manual parallel work should too.
- **Power-first agent dispatch.** Default to `ddx run`/`ddx try`/`ddx work`
  with power bounds. `--harness`, `--provider`, and `--model` are passthrough
  constraints only; DDx must not use them for routing policy.

## Links out

- Full CLI reference: `ddx --help`, `ddx <subcommand> --help`.
- Governing feature specs: see `FEAT-*` documents under your
  project's `docs/` tree — especially the CLI, beads, agent-service,
  executions, and skills features.
- Personas README: shipped by the default `ddx` plugin at
  `.ddx/plugins/ddx/personas/README.md`.
- Open standard this skill conforms to:
  [agentskills.io](https://agentskills.io).

# Work — Drain the Queue, Execute, Verify, Close

"Doing work" in DDx means draining the ready queue: pick the top ready bead,
run one or more `ddx try` attempts, verify the result, and close the bead on
success or leave it available for a future eligible retry.

## Default surface: `ddx work`

```bash
ddx work
```

`ddx work` drains the queue by picking ready beads and invoking `ddx try`.
`ddx try` wraps `ddx run`, which is the single agent invocation primitive. DDx
owns queue iteration, attempt evidence, and retry policy; the upstream agent
owns provider/model routing.

Flags worth knowing:

- `--once` — pick one bead and stop (don't loop).
- `--poll-interval <dur>` — continuous worker mode; wait between
  iterations.
- `--min-power <n>` / `--max-power <n>` — requested agent power bounds.
- `--top-power` — choose a `MinPower` threshold from the agent catalog.
- `--harness <name>` / `--provider <name>` / `--model <ref>` — passthrough
  constraints only. DDx sends them unchanged and does not route on them.

## Primitive: `ddx try`

For targeted re-runs, debugging, or running a specific bead:

```bash
ddx try <bead-id>
ddx try <bead-id> --from <rev>      # base commit override
ddx try <bead-id> --no-merge        # preserve result, don't land
```

`ddx try` runs one bead attempt in an isolated git worktree. It calls `ddx run`
for the actual agent invocation.

## Pick by default, not by ID

Under normal operation, **don't specify a bead ID**. `ddx work` picks
the top ready bead based on priority + dependency satisfaction. Only
pin a specific ID when debugging (`ddx try <id>`) or
when the queue ordering would pick the wrong bead and you need to
override.

## Verify independently before closing

**Agents hallucinate successful completions.** Do not trust the
agent's self-report. Before closing a bead:

1. Run the acceptance-criteria command yourself (from the bead's
   `accept:` field). If it's `go test ./foo/...`, run that.
2. Check the resulting commit against the in-scope file list from
   the bead description. Out-of-scope files touched? Reject the
   attempt.
3. Read the commit message — does it reference the bead ID?
   (`[ddx-<id>]` or similar in the commit subject is the convention.)

If all three pass: close the bead.

## Close on success, unclaim on failure

DDx try/work outcomes form a specific taxonomy. Each outcome
maps to a concrete follow-up action:

| Outcome | Meaning | Action |
|---|---|---|
| `success` | Tests pass, AC met, commit landed | `ddx bead close <id>` |
| `already_satisfied` | No changes needed (AC was already green) | `ddx bead close <id>` |
| `no_changes` | Agent returned without producing commits and wrote `no_changes_rationale.txt` | Leave open, **unclaim** |
| `no_evidence_produced` | Agent exited without a commit or rationale | Leave open, **unclaim**, investigate harness/commit failure |
| `land_conflict` | Merge conflict on landing the result | Leave open, **unclaim** |
| `post_run_check_failed` | Tests or gate failed after landing | Leave open, **unclaim**, investigate |
| `execution_failed` | Agent subprocess errored (timeout, crash, provider error) | Leave open, **unclaim** |
| `structural_validation_failed` | Result failed structural sanity check | Leave open, **unclaim**, investigate |

`ddx work` applies these actions automatically. If you're running
`ddx try` directly, apply them manually: `ddx bead update <id>
--unclaim` to release a bead after a non-closing outcome, so another
worker can pick it up.

**Never leave a bead half-owned** — every execution either closes or
unclaims.

## Testing expectations for bead implementations

When an agent implements a bead, its output should include tests
that exercise the new code:

- **Unit tests** for logic with in-memory stubs for first-party
  collaborators. Favor stubs over mocks; mocks that assert on call
  sequences test implementation, not behavior.
- **Integration tests** against real collaborators where the cost
  is small (real git in a temp dir, real DB in a temp file, real
  HTTP via a local test server).
- **Real e2e tests** at the outermost boundary. E2e tests that mock
  the database or the network are unit tests lying about their
  scope.
- **Coverage measurement only where the project tracks it.** Don't
  introduce coverage tooling just for one bead; use what the
  project already has. Coverage is a signal, not a target.
- **Performance claims require baselines.** If the bead's AC
  mentions "faster" or "scales", the acceptance test must include:
  (a) a numeric baseline, (b) an explicit boundary (what's
  measured, what's excluded), (c) a reproducible harness.

## Anti-patterns

- **Trusting the agent's "done"**: always re-run the AC command
  yourself before closing.
- **Closing on no_changes/no_evidence**: only `success` and `already_satisfied`
  close a bead. `no_changes` requires an explicit rationale. `no_evidence_produced`
  means the agent returned without a commit or rationale — unclaim and investigate.
- **Squashing bead-attempt commits**: the per-attempt history is
  an audit trail (evidence commits, heartbeats). Use only
  `git merge --ff-only` or `--no-ff`; never squash/rebase/filter.
- **Running passthrough pins without a reason**: power-bound dispatch lets the
  agent choose an appropriate route. Use `--harness`, `--provider`, or
  `--model` only for explicit operator constraints, bug repros, or controlled
  tests.
- **Parallel workers on the same claimed bead**: the tracker
  guards against this via claim semantics, but don't try to defeat
  it — each claim represents an in-flight attempt.

## CLI reference

```bash
ddx work                                    # default queue drain
ddx work --once                             # one bead, then stop
ddx work --poll-interval 30s                # continuous worker
ddx work --min-power 10                     # request stronger attempts
ddx work --harness claude                   # passthrough constraint

ddx try <id>                                # one bead attempt
ddx try <id> --from <rev>                   # override base commit
ddx try <id> --no-merge                     # preserve iteration
```

Full flag list: `ddx work --help`, `ddx try --help`, `ddx run --help`.

./.agents/skills/.ddx/beads.jsonl:1:{"acceptance":"- [ ] WorkerManager starts a supervisor goroutine on first worker launch
- [ ] Watchdog reaps workers past `WatchdogDeadline` with no phase transition in `StallDeadline`
- [ ] SIGTERM→SIGKILL escalation works on an intentionally-wedged test worker (unit test with a goroutine that ignores ctx)
- [ ] Reaped worker's bead claim is released (tracker status returns to open or claim-expired)
- [ ] `bead.reaped` event recorded on tracker with reason="watchdog" and duration
- [ ] Watchdog deadlines are configurable via ddx server config","claimed-at":"2026-04-18T05:48:25Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"1833233","closing_commit_sha":"13cef95535aea82c1f00dd596704f141ed131af7","created_at":"2026-04-18T04:43:00.09109294Z","description":"## Residual risk after RC1–RC4 from ddx-0a651925

Even with context propagation (RC1), wall-clock bounds (RC2), tightened breakers (RC3), and provider deadlines (RC4), a goroutine-level deadlock or panic-swallowed handler could still leave a worker alive past any reasonable bound. Today, `server.WorkerManager.Stop` (workers.go:648) only calls `handle.cancel()` — it has no escalation to OS-level signal and no autonomous trigger. ddx-b808df39 adds the *operator* tool; this bead adds the *autonomous* watchdog.

## Scope

1. WorkerManager runs a supervisor goroutine that, every minute, inspects each `workerHandle`.
2. If `time.Since(record.StartedAt) > WatchdogDeadline` (default e.g. 6h) AND `record.CurrentAttempt` has not transitioned in `StallDeadline` (default e.g. 1h), escalate:
   - Send SIGTERM to the worker's process group (requires surfacing PID, aligned with ddx-b808df39).
   - After 30s grace, send SIGKILL.
   - Mark record.State = "reaped"; update claimed bead state so the claim is not leaked.
3. Emit a `bead.reaped` event on the tracker with the full timeline so post-mortems are easy.

## Non-goals

- Per-bead runtime tuning; defaults are enough.
- Replacing ddx-b808df39 — this is defense-in-depth, not duplication. ddx-b808df39 is operator-initiated; this bead is system-initiated.

## Dependencies

- ddx-b808df39 surfaces PID in `ddx agent workers --json` output; this bead reuses that Provider call returns within deadline with a distinct error","claimed-at":"2026-04-18T05:30:50Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"1833233","closing_commit_sha":"b0a4fce4796e6841c9a9686ebeee197fb22a2fa7","created_at":"2026-04-18T04:42:38.821147268Z","description":"## Root cause RC4 from ddx-0a651925

After RC1 is fixed (caller ctx propagates), provider HTTP calls still need per-request deadlines. A stalled TCP socket that delivers headers and then stops sending body bytes — particularly during streaming token reads — can hang a goroutine for days.

Grepping the `DocumentDrivenDX/agent@v0.3.14` module cache shows short timeouts only on discovery probes (`provider/openai/discovery.go:46,174`, `openai.go:123`). The main `Run` call path inside agent providers appears to inherit whatever ctx DDx passes — which today is `context.Background()` (see RC1).

## Scope

1. Audit `openai`, `anthropic`, `virtual` providers in the agent library for per-request deadline usage.
2. Install a per-request deadline (default ~10-15 min) on each provider call; separate from the wall-clock bound in RC2.
3. For streaming bodies, enforce an **idle-read limit** on the response body so a mid-stream stall cannot hold a goroutine indefinitely (e.g. `http.Transport.ResponseHeaderTimeout` + periodic `SetReadDeadline` on the underlying conn).
4. Surface provider-timeout failures as a distinct status in `ExecuteBeadResult.Detail`.

## Non-goals

- Rewriting providers; minimally invasive change only.
- Changing DDx-side timeout defaults (RC2 covers outer bound).

## Prerequisite

Sibling RC1 bead must be resolved first, otherwise adding deadlines inside providers is useless while DDx still feeds them ./.agents/skills/.ddx/beads.jsonl:3:{"acceptance":"- [ ] `RunOptions` accepts a caller ctx, or `Runner.Run`/`Runner.RunAgent` accept ctx explicitly
- [ ] The internal cancel is derived from the caller ctx (no `context.Background()` seeds inside Run/RunAgent)
- [ ] `agent.ExecuteBead` takes ctx and passes it down
- [ ] `server.WorkerManager.Stop(id)` cancels the running agent provider call within 2s in a unit test (fake provider blocks on ctx)
- [ ] Regression test: runner with a canceled ctx returns promptly (<500ms) regardless of mocked provider latency","claimed-at":"2026-04-18T04:51:41Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"1833233","closing_commit_sha":"9b044bcebc981533d2d48d4ffd79a91743dee884","created_at":"2026-04-18T04:41:24.464808955Z","description":"## Root cause RC1 from ddx-0a651925

`cli/internal/agent/runner.go:239` (Runner.Run) and `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:163` (Runner.RunAgent) both call `context.WithCancel(context.Background())`, discarding any context from the caller. `RunOptions` exposes no ctx field. `agent.ExecuteBead` likewise takes no ctx, so `server.WorkerManager.runWorker`'s ctx never reaches the agent call.

As a result, `WorkerManager.Stop(id)` (server/workers.go:648) cancels only the worker goroutine; the embedded `agentlib.Run` call (and any subprocess it launched) keeps running until SIGKILL. This is the structural defect behind 33–142h hung workers.

## Scope

1. Add `Context context.Context` to `RunOptions` (or change signatures to `Run(ctx, opts)`). Default to `context.Background()` when nil.
2. Derive the internal ctx from the caller's rather than `context.Background()`.
3. Thread ctx through `agent.ExecuteBead` → `runner.Run` → `agentlib.Run` → provider HTTP.
4. Update `server.WorkerManager.runWorker`'s `singleTierAttempt` closure to pass `ctx` into `agent.ExecuteBead`.

## Non-goals

- New wall-clock timeout logic (covered by sibling RC2 bead)

Regression anchor for ddx-0a651925 RC1: runner.Run and\n+// Runner.RunAgent previously discarded the caller's ctx by constructing\n+// their own context.WithCancel(context.Background()), so WorkerManager.Stop\n+// could not cancel the in-flight agent call.\n+func TestRunAgentCancelledContextReturnsPromptly(t *testing.T) {\n+\tprovider := &sleepProvider{\n+\t\tdelay: 30 * time.Second, // far larger than the 500ms AC deadline\n+\t\tresponse: agentlib.Response{\n+\t\t\tContent: \"never-returned\",\n+\t\t\tModel:   \"test-model\",\n+\t\t},\n+\t}\n+\n+\tr := NewRunner(Config{SessionLogDir: t.TempDir()})\n+\tr.LookPath = mockLookPath\n+\tr.AgentProvider = provider\n+\n+\tctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(context.Background())\n+\tcancel() // pre-cancel so the call returns on entry\n+\n+\tstart := time.Now()\n+\tresult, err := r.RunAgent(RunOptions{\n+\t\tHarness: \"agent\",\n+\t\tPrompt:  \"test\",\n+\t\tContext: ctx,\n+\t})\n+\telapsed := time.Since(start)\n+\n+\trequire.NoError(t, err)\n+\trequire.NotNil(t, result)\n+\tassert.Equal(t, 1, result.ExitCode, \"canceled run should report non-zero exit\")\n+\tassert.NotEmpty(t, result.Error, \"canceled run should carry error text\")\n+\tassert.Less(t, elapsed, 500*time.Millisecond,\n+\t\t\"canceled ctx must abort the provider call promptly; took %v\", elapsed)\n+}\n+\n+// TestRunAgentContextCancelledMidRunReturnsPromptly cancels the caller's\n+// context while the provider is blocked, then asserts that the runner\n+// unwinds within 500ms of cancellation. This exercises the propagation\n+// path (caller ctx → internal WithCancel(parent) → agentlib.Run → provider\n+// Chat) rather than the short-circuit pre-canceled case.\n+func TestRunAgentContextCancelledMidRunReturnsPromptly(t *testing.T) {\n+\tprovider := &sleepProvider{\n+\t\tdelay: 30 * time.Second,\n+\t\tresponse: agentlib.Response{\n+\t\t\tContent: \"never-returned\",\n+\t\t\tModel:   \"test-model\",\n+\t\t},\n+\t}\n+\n+\tr := NewRunner(Config{SessionLogDir: t.TempDir()})\n+\tr.LookPath = mockLookPath\n+\tr.AgentProvider = provider\n+\n+\tctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(context.Background())\n+\t// Cancel after a brief warmup so we measure the cancel→return latency.\n+\tgo func() {\n+\t\ttime.Sleep(50 * time.Millisecond)\n+\t\tcancel()\n+\t}()\n+\n+\tstart := time.Now()\n+\tresult, err := r.RunAgent(RunOptions{\n+\t\tHarness: \"agent\",\n+\t\tPrompt:  \"test\",\n+\t\tContext: ctx,\n+\t})\n+\telapsed := time.Since(start)\n+\n+\trequire.NoError(t, err)\n+\trequire.NotNil(t, result)\n+\tassert.Equal(t, 1, result.ExitCode)\n+\tassert.Less(t, elapsed, 500*time.Millisecond,\n+\t\t\"caller cancel must propagate within 500ms; took %v\", elapsed)\n+}\n+\n+// TestRunCancelledContextReturnsPromptlySubprocess verifies the same\n+// cancellation contract for the subprocess dispatch path through Runner.Run.\n+// The OSExecutor honours ctx by sending SIGKILL on ctx.Done, so a canceled\n+// ctx must not leave the runner blocked on the child exit.\n+func TestRunCancelledContextReturnsPromptlySubprocess(t *testing.T) {\n+\t// Use the mockExecutor (in-process fake) with a blocking delay. A\n+\t// canceled context should short-circuit execution before any exec\n+\t// happens, rather than waiting for the mock.\n+\texec := &blockingMockExecutor{block: 30 * time.Second}\n+\tr := newTestRunner(&mockExecutor{output: \"ok\"})\n+\tr.Executor = exec\n+\n+\tctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(context.Background())\n+\tcancel()\n+\n+\tstart := time.Now()\n+\tresult, err := r.Run(RunOptions{\n+\t\tHarness: \"codex\",\n+\t\tPrompt:  \"hello\",\n+\t\tContext: ctx,\n+\t})\n+\telapsed := time.Since(start)\n+\n+\trequire.NoError(t, err)\n+\trequire.NotNil(t, result)\n+\tassert.Less(t, elapsed, 500*time.Millisecond,\n+\t\t\"canceled ctx must abort subprocess dispatch promptly; took %v\", elapsed)\n+}\n+\n+// blockingMockExecutor is an Executor whose ExecuteInDir respects ctx: it\n+// either blocks for `block` or returns ctx.Err() when ctx is canceled.\n+type blockingMockExecutor struct {\n+\tblock time.Duration\n+}\n+\n+func (e *blockingMockExecutor) Execute(ctx context.Context, binary string, args []string, stdin string) (*ExecResult, error) {\n+\treturn e.ExecuteInDir(ctx, binary, args, stdin, \"\")\n+}\n+\n+func (e *blockingMockExecutor) ExecuteInDir(ctx context.Context, _ string, _ []string, _ string, _ string) (*ExecResult, error) {\n+\tselect {\n+\tcase <-time.After(e.block):\n+\t\treturn &ExecResult{Stdout: \"\", ExitCode: 0}, nil\n+\tcase <-ctx.Done():\n+\t\treturn &ExecResult{ExitCode: -1}, ctx.Err()\n+\t}\n+}\ndiff --git a/cli/internal/server/workers_stop_propagation_test.go b/cli/internal/server/workers_stop_propagation_test.go\nnew file mode 100644\nindex 00000000..4b7ce402\n--- /dev/null\n+++ b/cli/internal/server/workers_stop_propagation_test.go\n@@ -0,0 +1,86 @@\n+package server\n+\n+import (\n+\t\"context\"\n+\t\"sync/atomic\"\n+\t\"testing\"\n+\t\"time\"\n+\n+\t\"github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/ddx/internal/agent\"\n+\t\"github.com/stretchr/testify/assert\"\n+\t\"github.com/stretchr/testify/require\"\n+)\n+\n+// TestWorkerManagerStopCancelsAgentExecution is the regression test for\n+// ddx-0a651925 RC1: WorkerManager.Stop(id) must cancel the in-flight\n+// executor (and transitively the agent provider call) via ctx propagation,\n+// not merely close the poll loop. Pre-fix, runner.Run / RunAgent discarded\n+// the caller ctx by using context.WithCancel(context.Background()), so\n+// cancelling the worker goroutine left the agent subprocess / HTTP call\n+// running until SIGKILL. With ctx threaded into RunOptions.Context, Stop's\n+// cancellation propagates into the executor within seconds.\n+//\n+// The fake executor here blocks on ctx (exactly like a real provider's\n+// Chat method), so a successful test proves cancellation reaches the\n+// provider boundary.\n+func TestWorkerManagerStopCancelsAgentExecution(t *testing.T) {\n+\troot := t.TempDir()\n+\tsetupBeadStoreWithReadyBead(t, root)\n+\n+\tvar executorStarted atomic.Bool\n+\tvar executorCtxErr atomic.Value // stores the ctx.Err() observed when the executor unblocks\n+\texecutorDone := make(chan struct{})\n+\n+\tm := NewWorkerManager(root)\n+\tm.BeadWorkerFactory = func(s agent.ExecuteBeadLoopStore) *agent.ExecuteBeadWorker {\n+\t\treturn &agent.ExecuteBeadWorker{\n+\t\t\tStore: s,\n+\t\t\tExecutor: agent.ExecuteBeadExecutorFunc(func(ctx context.Context, beadID string) (agent.ExecuteBeadReport, error) {\n+\t\t\t\texecutorStarted.Store(true)\n+\t\t\t\t// Block on ctx — mirrors what a real agent provider HTTP call\n+\t\t\t\t// does when ctx is threaded all the way down.\n+\t\t\t\tselect {\n+\t\t\t\tcase <-ctx.Done():\n+\t\t\t\t\texecutorCtxErr.Store(ctx.Err())\n+\t\t\t\tcase <-time.After(60 * time.Second):\n+\t\t\t\t\t// If we get here, ctx cancellation never propagated.\n+\t\t\t\t}\n+\t\t\t\tclose(executorDone)\n+\t\t\t\treturn agent.ExecuteBeadReport{\n+\t\t\t\t\tBeadID: beadID,\n+\t\t\t\t\tStatus: agent.ExecuteBeadStatusExecutionFailed,\n+\t\t\t\t\tDetail: \"canceled\",\n+\t\t\t\t}, nil\n+\t\t\t}),\n+\t\t}\n+\t}\n+\n+\trecord, err := m.StartExecuteLoop(ExecuteLoopWorkerSpec{\n+\t\tPollInterval: 30 * time.Second,\n+\t})\n+\trequire.NoError(t, err)\n+\n+\t// Wait until the executor has actually started running so we know the\n+\t// cancellation we exercise is against an in-flight call.\n+\trequire.Eventually(t, executorStarted.Load, 3*time.Second, 20*time.Millisecond,\n+\t\t\"executor never entered the blocking Chat call\")\n+\n+\tstopStart := time.Now()\n+\trequire.NoError(t, m.Stop(record.ID))\n+\n+\tselect {\n+\tcase <-executorDone:\n+\tcase <-time.After(2 * time.Second):\n+\t\tt.Fatalf(\"executor was not canceled within 2s of WorkerManager.Stop\")\n+\t}\n+\telapsed := time.Since(stopStart)\n+\tassert.Less(t, elapsed, 2*time.Second,\n+\t\t\"Stop should cancel the running agent call within 2s; took %v\", elapsed)\n+\n+\tobservedErr, _ := executorCtxErr.Load().(error)\n+\tassert.ErrorIs(t, observedErr, context.Canceled,\n+\t\t\"executor should observe context.Canceled from the caller ctx; got %v\", observedErr)\n+\n+\t// Ensure the worker goroutine also exits.\n+\t_ = waitForWorkerExit(t, m, record.ID, 5*time.Second)\n+}","is_error":false}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"8e7910e8-fc0d-48fa-99d0-f3a12ea80392","uuid":"3fe23d3a-4723-45b7-889a-0b90ca59dfda","timestamp":"2026-04-18T05:15:09.240Z","tool_use_result":{"stdout":"diff ./.agents/skills/.ddx/beads.jsonl:5:{"acceptance":"- [ ] New default wall-clock deadline (e.g. 3h) applied to both `OSExecutor.ExecuteInDir` and `Runner.RunAgent`
- [ ] Wall-clock timeout fires even when stdout/stderr/agent events are flowing (unit test: fake provider emits 1 event/sec for > deadline, expect cancel at deadline ± 1s)
- [ ] Idle timeout behaviour preserved (unit test: no activity for idle timeout → fire)
- [ ] Result.json distinguishes `timeout: wall-clock` from `timeout: idle`
- [ ] Documented in types.go (DefaultWallClockMS constant with comment)","claimed-at":"2026-04-18T05:15:25Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"1833233","closing_commit_sha":"fff2c86240b98cd687a9dc9879926b17411db3f5","created_at":"2026-04-18T04:41:40.15942901Z","description":"## Root cause RC2 from ddx-0a651925

The `DefaultTimeoutMS = 7200000` (2h) in `cli/internal/agent/types.go:363` is advertised as a hard cap but implemented as an *idle timeout*:

- `cli/internal/agent/executor.go:154-178` resets `time.NewTimer(idleTimeout)` on every stdout/stderr byte.
- `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:264-285` resets on every non-compaction agentlib event.

A provider that emits any heartbeat, retry line, or periodic event defeats the timer indefinitely — enabling the 33–142h hangs observed on agent projects.

Only the claude streaming path (`cli/internal/agent/claude_stream.go:373-384`) uses a true wall-clock deadline via `time.After(timeout)`. That path is why bundle `20260415T015231-c2ffa9ba` fired at exactly 2h 0m 0.098s — and nothing else does.

## Scope

1. Add an absolute wall-clock deadline in addition to (not replacing) the idle timeout.
2. Default the wall-clock bound to something operators can trust — e.g. 3x `DefaultTimeoutMS` or a new `DefaultWallClockMS`.
3. Apply in: `OSExecutor.ExecuteInDir` (subprocess path) and `Runner.RunAgent` (embedded agent path).
4. Reflect the wall-clock error in result.json as a distinct `error: "wall-clock deadline exceeded after Xh"` so it is not confused with idle timeout.

## Non-goals

- Lowering or removing the existing idle timeout.
- Per-provider tuning (can be This is the RC2 ddx-0a651925 regression guard: a provider\n+// that emits pulses forever must not be able to pin a worker indefinitely.\n+func TestAgentRunWallClockTimeout(t *testing.T) {\n+\twd := t.TempDir()\n+\n+\t// pulseProvider keeps emitting a fresh read tool-call every delay ms.\n+\t// Each response produces llm-request + tool-call events that would\n+\t// otherwise reset the idle-timer forever.\n+\tprovider := &pulseProvider{\n+\t\tdelay:    50 * time.Millisecond,\n+\t\tpathBase: \"missing\",\n+\t}\n+\n+\tr := NewRunner(Config{\n+\t\tSessionLogDir: t.TempDir(),\n+\t\tTimeoutMS:     5000, // idle timer is generous — should never fire in this test\n+\t\tWallClockMS:   300,  // wall-clock cap is short — must fire\n+\t})\n+\tr.LookPath = mockLookPath\n+\tr.AgentProvider = provider\n+\n+\tstart := time.Now()\n+\tresult, err := r.RunAgent(RunOptions{\n+\t\tHarness: \"agent\",\n+\t\tPrompt:  \"loop forever\",\n+\t\tWorkDir: wd,\n+\t})\n+\telapsed := time.Since(start)\n+\n+\trequire.NoError(t, err)\n+\tassert.Equal(t, 1, result.ExitCode)\n+\tassert.Contains(t, result.Error, \"wall-clock deadline exceeded\",\n+\t\t\"expected wall-clock error, got %q\", result.Error)\n+\tassert.GreaterOrEqual(t, elapsed, 250*time.Millisecond,\n+\t\t\"wall-clock should not fire before its deadline\")\n+\tassert.Less(t, elapsed, 2*time.Second,\n+\t\t\"wall-clock should fire promptly after its deadline\")\n+\t// Sanity check: the provider must have actually emitted events during\n+\t// the wait, proving we defeated the idle timer rather than sitting idle.\n+\tassert.Greater(t, provider.calls.Load(), int32(1),\n+\t\t\"pulse provider should have emitted multiple events during the wait\")\n+}\n+\n+// TestAgentRunIdleTimeoutPreserved ensures the existing idle-timeout path\n+// still fires when the provider sits silent. ./.agents/skills/.ddx/beads.jsonl:35:{"acceptance":"**User story:** As a developer reviewing how different harness/provider/model combinations are performing, I expect the Efficacy page to load quickly on a workspace with tens of thousands of sessions, and I expect the rows to reflect every agent invocation — not just those tied to closed beads.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. **Source change.** `EfficacyRows` resolver reads from the sharded sessions index (`.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/sessions-YYYY-MM.jsonl`, per `ddx-2ceb02fa`). Bead-event read paths (`efficacyFingerprint`, `buildEfficacySnapshot`'s event loop, `attemptsFromEvidence`, `efficacyMemo`) are removed in the same PR — no dead code retained.

2. **Schema parity.** Row shape and field names stay as they are today (rowKey, harness, provider, model, attempts, successes, successRate, medianInputTokens, medianOutputTokens, medianDurationMs, medianCostUsd, warning). UI does not need to change. Optional new filters (`since`, `until`, `projectId`) are added as nullable args with sensible defaults.

3. **Fixture + baseline recorded.** Fixture seeds ≥10,000 session index rows across 2+ monthly shards with ≥10 distinct `(harness, provider, model)` groups. Baseline latencies (before any optimization beyond source change) written to bead notes with hardware + go-version footer.

4. **First-pass perf targets.**
   - All-time rollup on 10k-row, 2-shard fixture: p95 ≤ 150ms in-process / ≤ 400ms HTTP.
   - Last-30-days rollup on same fixture: p95 ≤ 60ms / ≤ 200ms HTTP.
   - Stretch fixture (50k rows × 24 shards): all-time p95 ≤ 400ms in-process / ≤ 1000ms HTTP, or **user is consulted** before choosing a new substrate.

5. **DB-decision protocol (per user directive 2026-04-22).**
   - If targets in (4) cannot be met with in-place optimization (streaming aggregation, per-shard memoization with correct invalidation, selective field read), the PR author posts a short proposal in the bead notes: measured achievable numbers, proposed substrate from {axon, sqlite, postgres}, and migration cost. Implementation of that substrate requires explicit user approval before landing.

6. **Completeness test.** A fixture seeds sessions that would not appear under the old bead-event path (open-bead sessions, `ddx agent run` sessions, benchmark sessions). The new resolver returns rows for each of them. A matching integration test on the old code path (kept as a golden-output comparison for one cycle) asserts the new output is a strict superset in the cases it should be.

7. **Freshness.** A session written via the sessions index writer (from `ddx-2ceb02fa`) is visible in the Efficacy query within 2s. Integration test writes a row and queries the aggregation.

8. **Playwright smoke.** `/efficacy` opens, renders the rollup table, and clicks into `EfficacyAttempts` for one row — all within normal navigation budgets. Uses the 10k-row fixture; asserts the table has ≥5 rows rendered.

9. **Cross-reference.** This bead is a dependent of `ddx-2ceb02fa` (sessions index must exist). If `ddx-2ceb02fa` is not yet landed when this work starts, bead notes record the stub / interim source chosen and the timeline for switching over.","claimed-at":"2026-04-23T05:29:15Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"3704155","closing_commit_sha":"9ee64108c2ca79e70a9dd5971a72665865469f2a","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:47:04.443214848Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ddx-0a33bc5f","depends_on_id":"ddx-2ceb02fa","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-23T01:21:53Z"}],"description":"## Observed

The Efficacy page at `/nodes/.../projects/.../efficacy` loads very slowly. User feedback: efficacy should really be "an aggregation of sessions grouped by provider / harness / model" — which is not what the current implementation does.

## Ground truth (current implementation)

`cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_feat008.go:676-833` —

- `EfficacyRows` builds its rollup from **bead evidence events** (`kind:routing` and `kind:cost` on every closed bead), not from sessions.
- Computation path: `store.ReadAll()` → for every closed bead, read its event file → filter for routing/cost → per-(harness, provider, model) aggregate.
- There is a cache (`efficacyMemo`), keyed by a fingerprint that itself walks every event on every closed bead to compute event count + max timestamp. So cache validation costs almost as much as cache rebuild.
- Cost shape: `O(closed_beads × events_per_bead)` on every request (cache miss) and even on cache hit the fingerprint walk happens.

## Why this is wrong (per user feedback)

- Efficacy is a sessions-view concept: "how is harness/provider/model X doing?" Sessions are the natural row. Bead events are a lossy, indirect proxy — they only exist for *closed* beads, they duplicate session state, and they force coupling between the efficacy feature and bead lifecycle.
- The bead-event path misses: open beads with in-flight sessions, sessions that didn't close a bead, benchmark/quorum sessions, non-execute-bead invocations (e.g., `ddx agent run`).
- The sessions feed is already being reworked into a sharded pointer index (`ddx-2ceb02fa`). Efficacy should read from that index — single source of truth.

## Proposed direction

### Part 1 — Reshape the data source

- Rebuild `EfficacyRows` as an aggregation over `sessions.index` (the sharded index from `ddx-2ceb02fa`), not over bead events.
- Grouping key: `(harness, provider, model)` — optionally filterable by project, bead, label, time range.
- Metrics per row: attempts (count), successes (count + rate), median input/output tokens, median duration, median cost. Identical shape to today; only the source changes.
- Shard awareness: time-range queries map to a shard set (same pattern as `ddx-2ceb02fa`); the default view reads current + previous shard.

### Part 2 — Fixture + baseline (honor user's 1,000+ record floor)

- Perf harness target: aggregate ≥**1,000 session rows** (≥ 10 distinct `(harness, provider, model)` groups) within the budget below.
- Stretch fixture: 50,000 session rows across 24 monthly shards. Measures steady-state behavior as the index grows.
- Baseline captured in the bead notes before any optimization.

### Part 3 — Perf targets (first pass, in-place)

- Default Efficacy view (all-time) on a 10k-session, 2-shard fixture: p95 ≤ 150ms in-process / ≤ 400ms HTTP.
- Date-filtered view (last 30 days, 1 shard): p95 ≤ 60ms in-process / ≤ 200ms HTTP.
- If in-place optimization (scope, pushdown, memoize-per-shard, streaming aggregation) **cannot hit these numbers** on the 50k stretch fixture, **do not silently change substrate** — raise the decision with the user. Candidate backends named by the user: axon, sqlite, postgres. Bring a recommendation + rationale, not a fait accompli.

### Part 4 — Drop the bead-event read path

- After the rewrite, remove `efficacyFingerprint`, `buildEfficacySnapshot` event iteration, `attemptsFromEvidence`, and `efficacyMemo`. They become dead weight once the source moves.
- Evidence events on beads remain (they're bead audit trail, not efficacy data); nothing in bead lifecycle changes.

## Out of scope

- The `comparisons` / "compare arms" feature on this page (tracked separately — see companion bead).
- Project-scope filtering UI (the backend support can be added now; the UI widget is a follow-up).

The current worktree contains the sharded sessions index implementation at .ddx/agent-logs/sessions/sessions-YYYY-MM.jsonl, so this pass reads that substrate directly; no interim source or stub was used.

Source-change baseline before per-shard memoization: fixture seeds 10,000 session index rows across 2 monthly shards with 10 distinct harness/provider/model groups, plus stretch fixture of 50,000 rows across 24 monthly shards. Measured with go test ./internal/server/graphql -run TestEfficacyRowsDateFilterAndPerfTargets -count=1 -v.

Source-only results:
- 10k all-time in-process p95: 46.87735ms (target <=150ms)
- 10k all-time HTTP p95: 47.756933ms (target <=400ms)
- 10k last-30-days in-process p95: 24.356549ms (target <=60ms)
- 10k last-30-days HTTP p95: 25.628964ms (target <=200ms)
- 50k x 24-shard all-time in-process p95: 279.266233ms (target <=400ms)
- 50k x 24-shard all-time HTTP p95: 300.428036ms (target <=1000ms)

Final implementation adds shard-stat invalidated session-index memoization. Final measured results:
- 10k all-time in-process p95: 1.368873ms
- 10k all-time HTTP p95: 3.202329ms
- 10k last-30-days in-process p95: 1.742706ms
- 10k last-30-days HTTP p95: 2.049998ms
- 50k x 24-shard all-time in-process p95: 242.886602ms
- 50k x 24-shard all-time HTTP p95: 886.748µs

DB-decision protocol: in-place session-index aggregation met the first-pass and stretch targets, so no axon/sqlite/postgres substrate proposal is required in this pass. Race-detector runs skip the perf-threshold test because race instrumentation invalidates latency targets; non-race CI enforces them.

This bead closes as 'investigation complete; see child beads for fixes'. ddx-b808df39 references this bead's findings in its implementation notes.","claimed-at":"2026-04-18T04:31:48Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"1833233","closing_commit_sha":"1d0b3e373a618623062909694332536c74f2630a","created_at":"2026-04-18T03:01:36.338860593Z","description":"## Context

ddx-b808df39 (P0: ddx agent stop/kill command for hung workers) treats hung-worker remediation as an operational need, but does NOT investigate the UNDERLYING cause of the hangs. The feature is a useful escape hatch, but shipping it without understanding WHY workers hang means we'll keep needing the escape hatch.

Opus fresh-eyes review (2026-04-18) flagged: 'Hung workers for 33h–142h are an operational defect independent of root cause' — the feature bead is justified, but a SIBLING root-cause investigation is missing from the portfolio.

## Observed

The referenced 33h and 142h hang durations in ddx-b808df39 imply multiple incidents where an execute-bead worker process remained in-progress past any reasonable upper bound for a single bead. Needs to investigate:

1. Is the worker process alive but stuck in a tool call (bash subprocess that never returns)?
2. Is it alive but mid-LLM-call with no timeout (provider hang + no client-side deadline)?
3. Is it dead but the bead's claimed_at never cleared (tracker-side leak)?
4. Is the worktree locked or fsync'd in a way that prevents cleanup?

## Fix direction

This bead is an INVESTIGATION, not a fix. Deliverable is a short report that:

1. Reproduces or observes at least one hang incident and captures:
   - ps / pgrep state of the worker process tree
   - stack traces via 'go tool pprof' against the worker (if alive)
   - Timeline from .ddx/executions/<attempt>/embedded/*.jsonl (last event type + timestamp)
   - tracker claimed_at and retry_after state

2. Classifies the hang into one of the four categories above (or a new category).

3. Files concrete follow-up beads for each identified root cause:
   - Tool subprocess timeout if bash hangs
   - LLM client deadline if provider hangs
   - Claim expiry / heartbeat if tracker-side leak
   - File-lock / worktree cleanup if fs-side

4. Recommends whether ddx-b808df39's stop/kill mechanism is sufficient as mitigation or needs additional primitives.

## Relation to ddx-b808df39

ddx-b808df39 ships the OPERATOR TOOL to stop a hung worker. This bead finds the BUG that causes hangs. Both are needed; ship ddx-b808df39 first if the operator-tool is more urgent, then this investigation.

## Non-goals

- Fixing the root cause in this bead. Scope is investigation + follow-up bead filing.
- Preventing all possible hangs (impossible). Scope is the actually-observed hang {"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01BWdc1gPDPzmZroZCAg9JbR","type":"tool_result","content":"commit 13cd213e157cd211c48f51796bd62a2fb8b1fcae\nAuthor: Test User <test@ddx.test>\nDate:   Sat Apr 18 00:49:37 2026 -0400\n\n    docs(agent): investigate root cause of 33–142h hung workers [ddx-0a651925]\n    \n    Root-cause investigation report for hung execute-bead workers observed on\n    agent projects (33h–142h). Updated\n    ddx-b808df39 notes to cross-reference this investigation so the\n    operator stop/kill tool tracks the underlying fixes.\n\ndiff --git a/.ddx/executions/20260418T043148-1346e8a3/incident_report.md b/.ddx/executions/20260418T043148-1346e8a3/incident_report.md\nnew file mode 100644\nindex 00000000..5e8547cf\n--- /dev/null\n+++ b/.ddx/executions/20260418T043148-1346e8a3/incident_report.md\n@@ -0,0 +1,193 @@\n+# Investigation: root cause of 33–142h hung execute-bead workers\n+\n+**Bead:** ddx-0a651925 (investigates; sibling to ddx-b808df39)\n+**Attempt:** 20260418T043148-1346e8a3\n+**Scope:** Source-code + execution-bundle analysis. No live hung worker was available\n+in this isolated worktree to capture live `ps`/`pprof`, so the incident evidence is\n+drawn from `.ddx/executions/*` bundles and the worker/runner source path.\n+\n+---\n+\n+## 1. Observed evidence (from `.ddx/executions/`)\n+\n+| Metric                                               | Count / value                                        |\n+| ---------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- |\n+| Total attempt bundles                                | 328                                                  |\n+| Bundles with `result.json`                           | 291 (89%)                                            |\n+| Bundles with `manifest.json` but **no** `result.json` | 37 (11%) — executions whose worker never finalized  |\n+\n+Top five longest *finalized* attempts:\n+\n+| `duration_ms` | Wall time    | `detail`                                                                    | Bundle                          |\n+| ------------- | ------------ | --------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------- |\n+| 7,720,908     | **2h 8m**    | `agent: compaction stuck: 50 consecutive failed compaction attempts`        | 20260416T230316-efbf91c5        |\n+| 7,200,098     | **2h 0m**    | `timeout after 2h0m0s` (claude streaming wall-clock fired)                  | 20260415T015231-c2ffa9ba        |\n+| 6,764,927     | **1h 53m**   | success (agent harness, qwen)                                               | 20260416T230315-f9f46822        |\n+| 5,649,987     | **1h 34m**   | compaction stuck                                                            | 20260416T230313-e193942b        |\n+| 4,284,347     | **1h 11m**   | compaction stuck                                                            | 20260415T103422-bb3a45fa        |\n+\n+**Key observation:** the 33h / 142h hang durations cited in ddx-b808df39 are\n+~15× and ~65× longer than the longest *finalized* attempt on record. This means\n+the hung workers never reached the point of writing `result.json` — they were\n+blocked inside `runner.Run` (or deeper) for days. Every code path that\n+*should* have terminated them (idle timeout, compaction-stuck, stall detector,\n+cancel propagation) failed to fire.\n+\n+The 37 manifest-only bundles are candidates for hung or externally-killed\n+workers; e.g. `20260416T215953-6b515c05` has a manifest from 2026-04-16T21:59Z\n+with no result. Those fit the profile of \"workers running 33h–142h with no bead\n+assigned\" reported on agent projects.\n+\n+---\n+\n+## 2. Root-cause classification (source code evidence)\n+\n+The investigation checked the four hypotheses from the bead description.\n+\n+### RC1 (PRIMARY) — Caller context is discarded inside the Runner\n+\n+`cli/internal/agent/runner.go:239` (`Runner.Run`):\n+```go\n+ctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(context.Background())\n+```\n+\n+`cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:163` (`Runner.RunAgent`):\n+```go\n+ctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(context.Background())\n+```\n+\n+`RunOptions` (types.go) takes no `context.Context` at all. `agent.ExecuteBead`\n+likewise does not accept ctx, and `server.WorkerManager.runWorker` passes its\n+own ctx into `worker.Run(ctx, …)` but that ctx **never reaches the agent\n+invocation**.\n+\n+**Impact:** `WorkerManager.Stop(id)` (workers.go:648) calls `handle.cancel()`\n+which cancels only the worker goroutine's ctx. The running agent provider call\n+/ subprocess is detached. The only way to interrupt a stuck agent today is a\n+SIGKILL from outside the process. This is the structural defect that enables\n+33–142h hangs — once the agent is stuck, nothing inside DDx can stop it.\n+\n+### RC2 — Idle-reset timer, not wall-clock deadline\n+\n+`cli/internal/agent/executor.go:35-40` creates an *idle timeout* context value:\n+```go\n+func withExecutionTimeout(ctx context.Context, timeout time.Duration) context.Context {\n+    return context.WithValue(ctx, executionTimeoutKey{}, timeout)\n+}\n+```\n+\n+`executor.go:154-178` consumes it and **resets the timer on every stdout/stderr\n+byte**. `agent_runner.go:264-285` implements the same pattern against agentlib\n+events.\n+\n+The default timeout is 2h (`types.go:363 DefaultTimeoutMS = 7200000`). The\n+comment claims \"2 hours — long enough for any task; supervisor/agent decides\n+termination\" but the actual behaviour is **\"2 hours of silence required to fire\"**.\n+A provider that emits a heartbeat, a ping, a retry line, or any byte resets the\n+timer indefinitely.\n+\n+The `runClaudeStreaming` path (`claude_stream.go:373-384`) is the only path that\n+uses a wall-clock deadline — and it is the only path we see actually hitting\n+the timeout (bundle 20260415T015231 exactly 2h 0m 0.098s).\n+\n+**Impact:** for `harness=agent` (qwen, minimax, openrouter, etc.), no wall-clock\n+bound exists. 33–142h hangs are consistent with a provider that dribbles events\n+slowly enough to defeat the idle reset.\n+\n+### RC3 — Compaction-stuck breaker fires late\n+\n+`cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:218-241` increments a counter on each no-op\n+compaction event and trips at `stuckThreshold = maxConsecutiveCompactionFailures`\n+(50 by default). On observed `efbf91c5` it took **2h 8m** to trip. If one\n+iteration takes 3 minutes to produce a no-op compaction event, 50 events =\n+~150 min — matches the observed tail.\n+\n+**Impact:** on its own, the breaker is too slow to be a useful hang-guard. It\n+should be replaced or complemented by a wall-clock-bounded variant.\n+\n+### RC4 — Provider HTTP requests likely lack a per-request deadline\n+\n+`RunAgent` passes the internal `context.Background()`-derived ctx into\n+`agentlib.Run(ctx, req)`. The agent library's providers (`openai.Client`,\n+anthropic client) do not appear to install a per-request deadline themselves\n+(grepping the v0.3.14 module cache shows short timeouts on discovery/probe\n+only; the main call path has no WithTimeout). A stalled TCP socket after\n+headers are received but before the response body arrives can hang a streaming\n+read indefinitely.\n+\n+**Impact:** consistent with \"alive but mid-LLM-call with no client-side\n+deadline\" — hypothesis (2) in the bead description. Cannot be fully confirmed\n+from source alone; needs runtime `go tool pprof http://localhost:<port>/debug/pprof/goroutine`\n+against a live hung worker.\n+\n+### RC-skipped — BashTool subprocess hang (hypothesis 1)\n+\n+Not the root cause. `github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent@v0.3.14/tool/bash.go:15`\n+installs a 120s default wall-clock timeout (`context.WithTimeout(ctx, timeout)`)\n+and calls `exec.CommandContext`. Per-call bash hangs self-terminate at 2min.\n+\n+### RC-skipped — Tracker-side claim leak (hypothesis 3)\n+\n+Tracker state is incidental to the hang: the bead's own description notes the\n+hung workers exist as processes (`ps aux | grep claude`). The worker process is\n+alive; the claim is alive because the worker is alive. Fixing the claim alone\n+does not kill the worker. This hypothesis is downstream, not upstream.\n+\n+### RC-skipped — Worktree file-lock (hypothesis 4)\n+\n+No evidence in bundles. Execute-bead worktrees are plain git worktrees at\n+`/tmp/ddx-exec-wt/*`; no process-held file locks would survive across 33–142h\n+without the owning process. If the process is alive, the lock is alive — same\n+as RC3.\n+\n+---\n+\n+## 3. Relationship to ddx-b808df39\n+\n+ddx-b808df39 proposes an operator `ddx agent workers stop <id>` command that\n+sends **SIGTERM at the OS level**, then SIGKILL after a grace period. That\n+design **does** work around RC1 (process-level signal bypasses the broken ctx\n+plumbing). So ddx-b808df39 is a *correct mitigation* — but only a mitigation.\n+\n+Without fixing RC1 (and at least RC2), operators will keep needing to invoke\n+`workers stop` manually. The autonomous remediations every other system relies\n+on (cancel ctx, idle timeout, circuit breaker) are each broken today in ways\n+that require explicit code changes, not just a new operator tool.\n+\n+**Recommendation:** Ship ddx-b808df39 first (quick operational relief), then\n+resolve the child beads filed below in order RC1 → RC2 → RC4 → RC3.\n+\n+---\n+\n+## 4. Follow-up beads filed\n+\n+Each addresses one root cause with a deterministic acceptance test. Priority\n+and ID are shown; full details available via `ddx bead show <id>`.\n+\n+The IDs below carry an `.execute-bead-wt-…` prefix because `ddx bead create`\n+was invoked from inside the isolated execute-bead worktree — this is a\n+separate DDx bug worth noting but orthogonal to the hang investigation. They\n+are parented to `ddx-0a651925` and resolvable by `ddx bead list --parent ddx-0a651925`.\n+\n+| RC  | Priority | Bead ID (suffix)                                                           | Title                                                              |\n+| --- | -------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------ |\n+| RC1 | P0       | `…-526efaf1` | propagate caller context through Runner.Run and RunAgent           |\n+| RC2 | P0       | `…-b203c889` | add wall-clock deadline alongside idle timeout for agent harness   |\n+| RC3 | P2       | `…-b0562380` | bound compaction-stuck breaker by wall-clock time                  |\n+| RC4 | P1       | `…-3e3913a0` | audit and enforce per-request HTTP deadlines on agent provider calls |\n+| —   | P1       | `…-34d039b7` | autonomous worker watchdog with process-level reaper (complements ddx-b808df39) |\n+\n+**Suggested execution order:** RC1 → RC2 → RC4 → RC3 → watchdog. RC1 is a\n+prerequisite for RC4 being meaningful.\n+\n+---\n+\n+## 5. What this investigation could NOT do\n+\n+- Capture live `ps`/`pprof` from a hung worker — none was accessible from the\n+  isolated execute-bead worktree. RC4 in particular needs a live `go tool pprof`\n+  goroutine dump to confirm that the stuck goroutine is in `net/http` streaming\n+  read, not some other path.\n+- Confirm whether the agent library's providers install any timeout that was\n+  missed in grep. The listed RC4 bead should start with a provider-path audit\n+  rather than an immediate fix.","is_error":false}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"7c4457b5-7817-4bd4-9850-94282c2cfdb7","uuid":"b9b08566-e074-49f2-a31f-e547a318b057","timestamp":"2026-04-18T04:51:23.303Z","tool_use_result":{"stdout":"commit 13cd213e157cd211c48f51796bd62a2fb8b1fcae\nAuthor: Test User <test@ddx.test>\nDate:   Sat Apr 18 00:49:37 2026 -0400\n\n    docs(agent): investigate root cause of 33–142h hung workers [ddx-0a651925]\n    \n    Root-cause investigation report for hung execute-bead workers observed on\n    agent projects (33h–142h). Root-cause summary:
- RC1 (PRIMARY): Runner.Run and Runner.RunAgent discard caller ctx (runner.go:239, agent_runner.go:163). WorkerManager.Stop cannot interrupt a running agent.
- RC2: idle-reset timer instead of wall-clock deadline for non-claude paths (executor.go:154, agent_runner.go:264).
- RC3: compaction-stuck breaker fires too late (~2h observed).
- RC4: provider HTTP calls likely lack per-request deadlines (confirm against live hung worker).
- BashTool subprocess hang ruled out (120s default timeout). Tracker-side / fs-lock hypotheses are downstream of RC1.

Follow-up beads filed (parent=ddx-0a651925):
- …-526efaf1 P0 [RC1] propagate caller context
- …-b203c889 P0 [RC2] wall-clock deadline
- …-3e3913a0 P1 [RC4] per-request HTTP deadlines
- …-b0562380 P2 [RC3] bound compaction-stuck breaker by wall-clock
- …-34d039b7 P1 autonomous worker watchdog (complements ddx-b808df39)

ddx-b808df39's operator stop/kill is a correct mitigation but NOT sufficient as sole fix — until RC1 lands, operators will keep needing it. Ship ddx-b808df39 first, then RC1 → RC2 → RC4 → RC3 → watchdog.

./.agents/skills/.ddx/beads.jsonl:73:{"acceptance":"`ddx agent execute-bead` resolves governing references for both doc-ID and repo-relative path-style `spec-id` values when the target file exists in the execution snapshot; the generated prompt/manifest include the resolved path reference instead of the `No governing references` fallback; and deterministic tests cover a bead whose `spec-id` is a repo-relative path such as `workflows/README.md`.","closing_commit_sha":"cee85e6565224b30b15f689fa7daac8ad5e4a12b","created_at":"2026-04-11T01:18:51.632271275Z","description":"<context-digest>
Alpha validation run from /Users/erik/Projects/helix using `ddx agent execute-loop --once --json` against a live HELIX bead whose `spec-id` is the repo-relative path `workflows/README.md`. Evidence includes `.ddx/executions/20260411T011535-bbda19d7/prompt.md`, `.ddx/executions/20260411T011535-bbda19d7/manifest.json`, and `cli/cmd/agent_execute_bead.go`.
</context-digest>

`ddx agent execute-bead` prompt synthesis failed to resolve a live path-style `spec-id` into governing references, so the generated execution prompt told the agent there were no governing references even though the referenced file exists in the execution snapshot.

## Reproduction
1. In `/Users/erik/Projects/helix`, inspect bead `helix-fef22846`; it carries `spec-id=workflows/README.md`.
2. Run `ddx agent execute-loop --once --json` or `ddx agent execute-bead helix-fef22846 --json`.
3. Open `.ddx/executions/20260411T011535-bbda19d7/prompt.md`.
4. Observe the prompt includes `- spec-id: workflows/README.md` but the `## Governing References` section says `No governing references were resolved from the current execution snapshot.`

## Observed gap
`executeBeadResolveGoverningRefs()` builds the doc graph and looks up only by graph document ID. Path-like `spec-id` values that point to real files in the repo are dropped instead of being resolved as governing references.

## Why this matters
This is exactly the context the execution prompt tells the agent to read before changing code or docs. When DDx drops an existing repo-relative path reference, agent execution becomes less grounded and the artifact bundle misstates the available governing context.","events":[{"actor":"erik","body":"cancelled

./.agents/skills/.ddx/beads.jsonl:99:{"acceptance":"**User story:** As an operator, I expect the Sessions page to reflect reality — if I ran `ddx work` today, today's sessions appear within seconds. And I expect the feed not to silently break again the next time the agent execution path is refactored.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. **Session-index source of truth.** A single writer (invoked from `service.Execute` or a thin wrapper that every production agent-execution path calls) appends one line per completed agent invocation to `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions.index.jsonl`. Fields are pointer-only: id, projectID, beadID, harness, surface, model, startedAt, endedAt, durationMs, cost, tokens, outcome, exitCode, bundlePath (relative), nativeLogRef (relative). No prompt or response bodies embedded.

2. **Single-call-site guarantee.** Unit test enumerates every production code path that executes an agent (execute-bead, execute-loop, `ddx agent run`, quorum, replay if still present) and asserts each appends exactly one index row. If a new path is added later without wiring this up, the test fails.

3. **Visibility restored.** GraphQL `sessions` query reads `sessions.index.jsonl`. Running `ddx work` (or any agent-execution path) against a fixture project causes the new session to appear in a GraphQL query within 2 seconds of completion. E2E test demonstrates this.

4. **Body lazy-load.** A new resolver `session(id: ID!) { prompt response stderr }` loads the heavy fields on demand from the execution bundle (`.ddx/executions/<ts>-<hash>/`) or the agent-log path, without reading every session in the index.

5. **Legacy migration.** A one-shot command (e.g. `ddx agent log reindex`) seeds the index from the existing `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions.jsonl` (397 MB corpus on the current repo), preserving historical sessions. Integration test uses a fixture `sessions.jsonl` and asserts the index has one row per legacy entry with all pointer fields populated.

6. **Deprecated-file behavior.** After migration, the legacy `sessions.jsonl` is not read by the server. `ddx agent usage` and related CLI commands (`cli/cmd/agent_usage.go:197,246,348`, `cli/cmd/status.go:179`) are updated to read from the index too, verified by their existing tests.

7. **Perf sanity.** The GraphQL `sessions` list resolves in ≤100ms p95 on a fixture with 10,000 index entries; current behavior times out or OOMs. Benchmark captured in bead notes as baseline + after-fix.

8. **UI note.** If there is a gap in the index (e.g. the 2026-04-19 → 2026-04-22 gap on the current repo, assuming backfill from executions/agent-logs is not feasible), the Sessions page surfaces a single-line banner "No sessions recorded between X and Y" so the gap is not silently invisible. If backfill is feasible, do it instead and skip the banner.

9. **Playwright e2e.**
   - Seeds a fixture project with `.ddx/executions/` bundles from both before and after a simulated gap.
   - Runs the migration command.
   - Opens `/sessions`, asserts the most recent session timestamp matches the latest bundle, asserts a clicked row lazy-loads prompt/response from the bundle rather than from an embedded payload.
   - Asserts the feed updates live (within 2s) after a new simulated session is written.","closing_commit_sha":"d733f7f20828708c5179f0ceff9462eaa34654eb","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:22:41.990684692Z","description":"## Observed

The Sessions page at `/nodes/.../projects/.../sessions` shows nothing newer than **2026-04-19**, three days ago. ddx is definitely still running — `.ddx/executions/` contains bundles from 2026-04-22 (today), `agent-s-*.jsonl` from 2026-04-21, `agent-loop-*.jsonl` from 2026-04-20. The UI simply cannot see them.

## Root cause

The Sessions GraphQL resolver reads exclusively from `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions.jsonl` (`cli/internal/server/state_graphql.go:504-516`). That file stopped being appended on 2026-04-19 19:46.

The only writer is `Runner.logSession` (`cli/internal/agent/runner.go:847-914`). It is called from four sites today:

- `cli/internal/agent/runner.go:229` (Runner.Run — legacy)
- `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner_service.go:216`
- `cli/internal/agent/claude_stream.go:516`
- `cli/internal/agent/virtual.go:206`

Production execute-bead / execute-loop no longer goes through any of them. The Runner-to-service refactor series (`5b933103`, `280f58e0`, `74eceda9`, `7cc1eb58`, `e05c785e`, `9f79f037`) routed the common path through `service.Execute` directly and did not carry forward the sessions.jsonl append. The last sessions.jsonl write lines up with the day that refactor set became default.

## Secondary issue (same fix should address)

`sessions.jsonl` is 397 MB for only 895 entries because each entry embeds the full `result.Output` (see `SessionEntry.Response` at `runner.go:879`). Growth is unbounded, there is no rotation, and `GetAgentSessionsGraphQL` (`state_graphql.go:476-490`) reads the whole file, parses every entry, and sorts in memory on every query. Even if we just restore the writer, the feed is fragile and will become a perf problem again quickly — relevant to `ddx-ad0db8fd` (bead detail was also O(N) scan).

## Proposed direction

Do not simply paper over by re-plumbing `logSession` into `service.Execute`. The right fix is:

1. **Decide the source of truth for "a session".** Current candidates on disk:
   - `.ddx/executions/<ts>-<hash>/{manifest.json,prompt.md,result.json}` — already written per execute-bead attempt.
   - `.ddx/agent-logs/agent-*.jsonl` and `agent-s-*.jsonl` — per-run streams from the agent harness.
   - Bead evidence events (kind:cost already written per attempt, see `execute_bead.go:492`).
   A session record should be derivable from a combination of execution bundles + agent-log streams, not from a separately-appended aggregate. The aggregate should be an **index**, not a **copy**.

2. **Introduce a lightweight index.** A thin `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions.index.jsonl` containing `{id, projectID, beadID, harness, model, startedAt, endedAt, durationMs, cost, tokens, outcome, bundlePath}` — pointer fields only, no embedded response bodies. Existing `sessions.jsonl` is deprecated to a read-only legacy file during a grace period.

3. **Write the index from `service.Execute`** (or a wrapper the execute-bead / run / execute-loop paths all share). One place to touch. Unit test guards that every production code path that executes an agent appends exactly one index row.

4. **GraphQL reads the index, streams bodies on demand.** `sessions { edges { node { ... } } }` reads the index. A new `session(id: ID!) { prompt, response, stderr }` lazy-loads the heavy fields from the execution bundle or agent-log file when the user expands a row.

5. **Legacy sessions.jsonl compatibility.** The index can be seeded once from the existing 397 MB file so historical data is not lost, then the old file is not read again. One-shot migration command (`ddx agent log reindex` or similar).

## Out of scope

- Replaying or regenerating sessions for the 2026-04-19 → 2026-04-22 gap. If the evidence exists in executions/agent-logs we can backfill the index; if not, accept the gap and cap it with a dated marker in the UI.
- A full telemetry rework. Summary
-------
All acceptance criteria for this bead are already satisfied at HEAD by prior merged commits:

  99033c89  fix: index agent sessions by monthly shard [ddx-2ceb02fa]
  4a721780  fix(perf): align agentSessions target with date-range GraphQL signature [ddx-2ceb02fa]
  d3b16629  test: lock session index project writer [ddx-2ceb02fa]

No additional code changes are required in this pass. The REVIEW:BLOCK entries
appended to the bead notes describe the original (pre-amendment) acceptance
text — in particular they assume a flat `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions.index.jsonl`
while the amendment ("2026-04-22 — User-directed amendment: shard the index")
explicitly supersedes that with monthly shards under
`.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/sessions-YYYY-MM.jsonl`. The current code follows the
amended shape.

What is done (AC-by-AC evidence against the amended contract)
-------------------------------------------------------------
AC 1 (amended) — Sharded index writer.
  cli/internal/agent/session_index.go
    - AppendSessionIndex(): single-writer; shard resolved from time.Now().UTC()
      via SessionIndexShardPath → sessions/sessions-YYYY-MM.jsonl.
    - SessionIndexEntry: pointer-only fields (no prompt/response/stderr bodies).
  Evidence test: TestSessionIndexWritesMonthlyShards (crosses a month boundary
  and asserts two shards).

AC 2 — Single-call-site guarantee.
  cli/internal/agent/service_run.go:225-226
    - RunViaServiceWith calls AppendSessionIndex once per invocation.
  cli/internal/agent/session_index_test.go
    - TestRunViaServiceWithAppendsOneSessionIndexRow: one call → one index row.
    - TestProductionAgentExecutionPathsUseIndexedServiceWriter: enumerates
      execute-bead, execute-loop, `ddx agent run`, quorum/compare, replay,
      and the service writer itself; fails if any path is not wired through
      RunViaService / RunViaServiceWith.

AC 3 — Visibility restored (GraphQL reads the index).
  cli/internal/server/state_graphql.go:630-690
    - GetAgentSessionsGraphQL → readProjectSessions → agent.ReadSessionIndex.
  Evidence tests (internal/server): TestGraphQLAgentSessions covers the
  resolver happy path; the e2e spec below asserts the live path.

AC 4 — Body lazy-load resolver.
  cli/internal/server/state_graphql.go:648-671
    - GetAgentSessionGraphQL(id) calls agent.FindSessionIndex + LoadSessionBodies.
  GraphQL schema:
    cli/internal/server/graphql/schema.graphql adds `agentSession(id: ID!)`
    with `prompt`, `response`, `stderr` fields.
  Evidence tests:
    - TestLoadSessionBodiesFromBundle (loads prompt/response/stderr from
      `.ddx/executions/<ts>-<hash>/` bundle artefacts).
    - e2e/sessions.spec.ts asserts the detail query fires on row click and
      shows bundle prompt/response in the expanded row.

AC 5 (amended) — Legacy migration shards too.
  cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1164-1177
    - `ddx agent log reindex` subcommand.
  cli/internal/agent/session_index.go:398-444
    - ReindexLegacySessions: splits entries by entry.Timestamp into monthly
      shards, dedupes on id, renames the legacy file to
      sessions.jsonl.legacy on first run so the server never reads it again.
  Evidence tests:
    - TestReindexLegacySessionsSplitsShardsAndDedupes (entries across
      Jan/Feb/Mar produce 3 shards; re-run count is 0).
    - TestAgentLogReindexMigratesLegacyFile (cmd-level integration).

AC 6 (amended) — Server reads the shard directory with date-range pushdown;
              deprecated-file read stops; agent usage / status updated.
  cli/internal/agent/session_index.go:371-396
    - SessionIndexShardFiles filters shards by [StartedAfter, StartedBefore).
  Evidence test: TestSessionIndexShardFilesSelectsOnlyIntersectingDateRange
    seeds 6 shards, asserts only 2 are read for a 2-month window.
  cli/cmd/agent_usage.go:191-284, 498-518
    - aggregateUsageFromSessionIndex and readUsageSessionIndexRecords read
      via agent.ReadSessionIndex, not the legacy jsonl.
  cli/cmd/status.go:173-179
    - collectStatusUsage uses aggregateUsageFromSessionIndex.
  Evidence tests: TestAgentUsage*, TestStatus* (all green).

AC 7 — Perf sanity: sharded read ≤50ms for 12×10k fixture.
  cli/internal/agent/session_index_test.go:241-313
    - BenchmarkReadSessionIndexDefaultWindow: 12 shards × 10k rows each,
      default window (current + previous shard).
    - BenchmarkLegacySessionsJSONLFullScan: legacy single-file full scan
      of the same corpus shape (2KB embedded response each), as a baseline.
  Captured baselines (already recorded in bead notes, 2026-04-23):
    - Legacy full scan+sort: 1_731_705_957 ns/op  (≈1.73s)
    - Sharded default window: 8_103_661 ns/op     (≈8.1ms) ← well under 50ms.
    - Environment footer: linux/arm64, Go go1.26.2.

AC 8 — UI gap banner.
  cli/internal/server/frontend/src/routes/nodes/[nodeId]/projects/[projectId]/sessions/+page.svelte
    - $derived recordingGap detects >24h gaps across the loaded sessions and
      surfaces a single-line amber banner ("No sessions recorded between …").
  Evidence: e2e/sessions.spec.ts asserts the banner renders when the mock
  feed contains a gap between 2026-04-18 and 2026-04-22.

AC 9 — Playwright e2e.
  cli/internal/server/frontend/e2e/sessions.spec.ts
    - Seeds two fixture rows spanning a multi-day gap.
    - Opens /sessions, asserts latest row (codex/gpt-5.4, 2026-04-22) is
      visible, gap banner is shown.
    - Clicks the row; asserts the lazy-body query fires (detailRequested)
      and bundle-sourced prompt/response bodies render.
    - Flips a flag to introduce a new session and asserts it appears within
      the 2-second poll window (invalidateAll every 2s in +page.svelte).

Test runs on this worktree
--------------------------
- go test ./internal/agent ./internal/server ./internal/server/graphql
  ./internal/processmetrics  — PASS
- go test ./cmd (skip the long-standing install acceptance tests, which fail
  because the test environment has no built ddx binary — unrelated to this
  bead) — PASS
- go test ./internal/server/perf — PASS (agentSessions harness runs green).

Why no commit this pass
-----------------------
Writing a "make the reviewer happy" literal `sessions.index.jsonl` path now
would unwind the amendment that the bead owner explicitly requested and would
reintroduce the single-file aggregate they asked us to split. That is not a
correct change. A no_changes attempt with the evidence above is the right
signal here.

Suggested follow-up
-------------------
If the next review still fires BLOCK, the review prompt should be grounded
against the amended acceptance criteria (notes section, 2026-04-22 amendment
— supersedes original AC 1/5/6/7). The amendment documents exactly why the
index is sharded under `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/` instead of a flat
`sessions.index.jsonl`.

correct change. A no_changes attempt with the evidence above is the right
signal here.

base_rev=d733f7f20828708c5179f0ceff9462eaa34654eb","created_at":"2026-04-23T04:18:24.141608308Z","kind":"execute-bead","source":"ddx agent execute-loop","summary":"already_satisfied"}],"execute-loop-last-detail":"escalation exhausted: unsupported model "minimax/minimax-m2.7" for harness "gemini"; supported models: gemini-2.5-pro, gemini-2.5-flash, gemini-2.5-flash-lite","execute-loop-last-status":"execution_failed","execute-loop-no-changes-count":1,"feature":"FEAT-010","id":"ddx-2ceb02fa","issue_type":"bug","labels":["feat-008","feat-010","feat-006","perf","observability"],"notes":"## 2026-04-22 — User-directed amendment: shard the index

Per user feedback: a single `sessions.index.jsonl` just replays the same problem at smaller scale. The aggregate needs to be split.

### Revised storage shape

- Directory: `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/` (per-project, under each registered project's `.ddx`).
- Inside: **monthly shards** named `sessions-YYYY-MM.jsonl`.
- Each shard is pointer-only, same fields as the previously-proposed index (id, projectID, beadID, harness, surface, model, startedAt, endedAt, durationMs, cost, tokens, outcome, exitCode, bundlePath, nativeLogRef).
- Writer resolves the target shard from `time.Now().UTC()` at append time and creates the shard file lazily on first write of the month.
- Heavy bodies (prompt, response, stderr) stay where they already live — `.ddx/executions/<ts>-<hash>/` and agent-log streams — and are lazy-loaded by the `session(id:)` resolver.

### Why monthly, not daily / size-based / SQLite

- **Daily** — produces tens of thousands of files over the project lifetime; ReadDir becomes the cost.
- **Size-based** — requires coordinating rotation with the append; race-prone and hard to test.
- **SQLite** — overkill for first pass; revisit if a single monthly shard grows past tens of MB.
- **Monthly** — ≤12 shards/year, human-readable filenames, date-range queries map trivially to shard selection, bounded shard size in practice.

### Reader behavior

- Default Sessions query loads the **current + previous shard** (covers "recent activity" for any current date).
- Date-range queries map the range to a shard set and read only those.
- Pagination beyond the default window triggers older-shard loads on demand.
- No shard-coordination lock needed: each month's file is append-only and only the current-month shard is actively written.

### Legacy migration

- `ddx agent log reindex` reads the existing `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions.jsonl` once and splits by each entry's `timestamp` field into the appropriate monthly shards under `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/`.
- After migration, the legacy file is renamed `sessions.jsonl.legacy` (not deleted) and never read again by the server. Hand-removal is the user's call.
- Idempotent: re-running the command merges without duplicating (index row dedup by `id`).

### Revised acceptance criteria (supersedes criteria 1, 5, 6, 7 on original description)

1. **Sharded index writer.** Single shared call site (from `service.Execute` or equivalent wrapper) appends one line to `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/sessions-YYYY-MM.jsonl`, computing the shard from `time.Now().UTC()`. Test: write across a simulated month boundary and assert two shards exist with the correct split.

5. **Legacy migration shards too.** `ddx agent log reindex` splits `sessions.jsonl` into monthly shards by entry timestamp. Integration test uses a fixture with entries across 3 distinct months and asserts 3 shards are produced with correct contents and no duplicates on re-run.

6. **Server reads the shard directory.** `GetAgentSessionsGraphQL` reads `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/*.jsonl`, respects any date-range arg, and loads only the shards that intersect the range. Unit test seeds 6 monthly shards and asserts a 2-month query reads exactly 2 files.

7. **Perf on sharded index.** Default Sessions view (current + previous shard) resolves in ≤50ms p95 on a fixture with 12 monthly shards × 10k entries each (120k total). Capture baseline (single-file read of same corpus) vs sharded in bead notes.

(Criteria 2, 3, 4, 8, 9 from the original acceptance list remain as written.)

### Scope clarifier

This amendment does not change the single-call-site writer guarantee or the lazy-body resolver — those remain the load-bearing ideas. The amendment is specifically about **how the pointer index is physically stored**.
## 2026-04-23 — Implementation benchmark and evidence

Baseline: `go test ./internal/agent -run '^$' -bench 'Benchmark(ReadSessionIndexDefaultWindow|LegacySessionsJSONLFullScan)$' -benchtime=1x` measured legacy single-file full scan/sort of 120k legacy-shaped rows with 2KB embedded responses at `BenchmarkLegacySessionsJSONLFullScan-12 1 1731705957 ns/op`.

After fix: the same command measured the sharded default-window reader at `BenchmarkReadSessionIndexDefaultWindow-12 1 8103661 ns/op` for 12 monthly shards x 10k rows each (120k total), with the default query reading only the current + previous shard window. Environment footer: linux/arm64, Go go1.26.2.
