On retry, DDx synthesizes a <bead-review> prompt whose <diff> section is the output of `git show <resultRev>`. When the prior attempt's landing sequence appended an evidence commit of the form `chore: add execution evidence [<attempt_id>]` that added `.ddx/executions/<attempt_id>/embedded/agent-*.jsonl` (full per-event NDJSON session log) as a tracked file, `resultRev` is that evidence commit's SHA (because `landEvidence` sets `result.NewTip = sha` and the orchestrator copies `land.NewTip` into `res.ResultRev`). `git show` of the evidence commit emits the entire session log diff with a `+` prefix per line. Session logs are 5-50k lines; the retry prompt exceeds every public context window.

## Category / severity / surface

- Category: correctness / resource-exhaustion
- Severity: high
- Surface: `ddx agent execute-bead` retry path triggered by `kind:review-malfunction` (and any other flow that re-synthesizes a <bead-review> prompt from a git diff/show since base_rev).

## Full bug description (verbatim)

On retry, DDx synthesizes a <bead-review> prompt whose <diff> section is the raw `git diff <base_rev>..<result_rev>` of the prior attempt. The prior attempt's landing sequence includes an evidence commit of the form:

    chore: add execution evidence [<attempt_id>]

that adds `.ddx/executions/<attempt_id>/embedded/agent-*.jsonl` — the full per-event NDJSON session log of the prior attempt — as a tracked file. Because that file is *added* in the diff range, the diff includes it in full with a `+` prefix on every line. Session logs are routinely 5-50k lines. One reasonable fix iteration is enough to push the retry prompt past 1-2 million tokens, which no currently supported model can accept — the provider streams back an n_keep > n_ctx error and the retry crashes without a request round-trip.

This is a self-poisoning loop: the "evidence" DDx commits to make attempts auditable is the exact artifact that makes the next attempt undeliverable.

## Reproduction (observed live 2026-04-17 in nexiq)

1. Bead `hx-dd4ab35f` claimed by execute-loop worker (harness=agent, model=qwen/qwen3.6-35b-a3b, provider=vidar).
2. Attempt `20260417T211600-d6a7dee6` succeeded and landed two commits: `cb5e733` (implementation + regression test [hx-dd4ab35f]) and `9a006dd` (chore: add execution evidence [20260417T211600-], adds `.ddx/executions/20260417T211600-d6a7dee6/embedded/agent-1776460560227794836.jsonl`, +20,623 lines).
3. Post-merge review emitted `BLOCK` without a rationale; DDx recorded `kind:review-malfunction` and reopened the bead for retry.
4. Retry `20260417T212928-27f9054a` synthesized `.ddx/executions/20260417T212928-27f9054a/prompt.md` of 6,307,997 bytes; `<bead-review>` block = 6,307,969 bytes; 99.9998% is the diff of the prior evidence commit, 99%+ of that diff is the session log file. Rest of <bead-review> (title/description/acceptance/labels/governing) is ~28 bytes.
5. lmstudio responded: "n_keep: 2,274,074 >= n_ctx: 196,608". Worker recorded failed attempt with zero usage. Same pattern on vidar (n_keep: 1,169,147) and bragi (n_keep: 2,274,074), all running qwen3.6-35b-a3b at max_context_length 262144. Provider/model is irrelevant — prompt exceeds every public context window.

## DDx code-path verification (Claude investigation 2026-04-17)

Relevant files and line ranges in /Users/erik/Projects/ddx:

- `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_review.go:553-561` — `DefaultBeadReviewer.gitShow(rev)` runs `git -C <projectRoot> show <rev>` with no pathspec. Output is injected raw into `<diff>` by `BuildReviewPrompt` at `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_review.go:383-384`.
- `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_review.go:427-431` — reviewer fetches `diff := r.gitShow(resultRev)` then builds the prompt.
- `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_land.go:540-555` (`landEvidence`) — creates `chore: add execution evidence [<short>]` and sets `result.NewTip = sha`.
- `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_land.go:428-434` — `RealLandingGitOps.StageDir` runs `git add -- <relPath>` on the entire `.ddx/executions/<attempt>/` directory with no exclusions for `embedded/`.
- `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_orchestrator.go:250-251` — after Land, `res.ResultRev = land.NewTip`, so `ResultRev` ends up pointing at the evidence commit. The reviewer then runs `git show <evidence-commit>`, which is literally the dump of the session log.
- `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go:1135-1164` (`VerifyCleanWorktree`) — also stages the evidence dir via `git add -- <evidenceDir>` and commits `chore: add execution evidence [<short>]`; same no-exclusion pattern.
- `cli/cmd/bead_review.go:162-171` (`beadReviewGitShow`) — mirror of the same bug in the standalone `ddx bead review` command.

Confirmation the bug reporter's claim about `:(exclude).ddx/executions/*/embedded`:
- `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go:269-283` (`SynthesizeCommit`) — the exclusion pathspec IS present here; the bug is that the evidence-landing commit path (`StageDir` + `VerifyCleanWorktree`) and the review path (`git show <rev>`) do not share this exclusion.
- `cli/cmd/init.go:19-46` — the gitignore rules DO include `.ddx/executions/*/embedded/`, but `git add -- <dir>` on pre-existing or explicitly-added paths can still stage, and older workspaces may pre-date this gitignore (matches nexiq's scenario where the evidence commit landed the session log).

## Deviation from bug description (minor)

The bug text describes the diff as `git diff <base_rev>..<result_rev>`. The code actually does `git show <resultRev>` (single-rev show). The self-poisoning mechanism is functionally identical: when `resultRev` points at the evidence commit (`landEvidence` + `res.ResultRev = land.NewTip`), `git show` emits the entire `+`-prefixed session log. All suggested fixes still apply.

## Suggested fixes (ascending scope)

1. Exclude execution-evidence paths from bead-review diffs by passing a pathspec to the `git show`/`git diff` call in the prompt builder: `-- . ':(exclude).ddx/executions/*/embedded/*' ':(exclude).ddx/executions/*/prompt.md' ':(exclude).ddx/executions/*/usage.json'`. The same exclusion already exists at `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go:269-283` for `SynthesizeCommit`; replicate it in `DefaultBeadReviewer.gitShow` (`execute_bead_review.go:553-561`), `beadReviewGitShow` (`cli/cmd/bead_review.go:162-171`), `landEvidence` → `StageDir` (`execute_bead_land.go:428-434,540-555`), and `VerifyCleanWorktree` (`execute_bead.go:1135-1164`).
2. Substitute, don't reproduce. When a tracked-file addition exceeds a byte/line budget in the diff, replace it with a one-line stub such as: `+++ b/.ddx/executions/.../agent-*.jsonl  (+20,623 lines of session evidence — omitted from review prompt)`.
3. Stop committing raw session logs as tracked evidence. Move `.ddx/executions/*/embedded/*.jsonl` to a gitignored store, or a separate `refs/ddx/evidence/*` ref analogous to `refs/ddx/iterations/*`.
4. Cap the <bead-review> prompt size with a hard budget (e.g., 150k tokens). Elide to summary + log warning when exceeded.

(1) is the minimal surgical fix; (3) is the correct long-term fix.

Add ddx agent metrics tier-success command that aggregates execution outcomes by harness/model tier.

## Context
Execution evidence result.json records harness, concrete model (via manifest.json or result.json), outcome (task_succeeded/task_failed/etc.), cost_usd, and duration_ms. This command aggregates those into tier-level success rates to inform routing policy.

## Files to touch
- cli/cmd/agent_metrics.go — new subcommand under ddx agent metrics
- cli/cmd/agent.go — register metrics subcommand group
- cli/cmd/agent_metrics_test.go — test with fixture execution dirs

## Implementation notes
- Scan .ddx/executions/*/result.json for harness and outcome fields
- Group by harness (or harness+model if useful) 
- Calculate success rate = count(task_succeeded) / count(all)
- Include avg cost and duration per tier","events":[{"actor":"ddx","body":"{"resolved_provider":"","fallback_chain":[]}","created_at":"2026-04-16T20:28:42.875894505Z","kind":"routing","source":"ddx agent execute-bead","summary":"provider="},{"actor":"ddx","body":"{"resolved_provider":"agent","resolved_model":"qwen/qwen3-coder-next","route_reason":"first-available","fallback_chain":[],"base_url":"http://vidar:1234/v1"}","created_at":"2026-04-16T21:25:12.642075237Z","kind":"routing","source":"ddx agent execute-bead","summary":"provider=agent model=qwen/qwen3-coder-next reason=first-available"},{"actor":"ddx","body":"{"resolved_provider":"claude","fallback_chain":[]}","created_at":"2026-04-16T21:37:09.264319783Z","kind":"routing","source":"ddx agent execute-bead","summary":"provider=claude"}],"id":"ddx-4cdb9600","issue_type":"task","labels":["phase:build","area:agent","area:cli","kind:measurement"],"parent":"ddx-4b59b816","priority":1,"status":"closed","title":"Tier success-rate analytics: ddx agent metrics tier-success","updated_at":"2026-04-16T21:49:28.836550287Z"}
./.agents/skills/.ddx/beads.jsonl:188:{"acceptance":"- [ ] Schema added to .ddx/config.yaml for executions.mirror (kind, path, include, async)
- [ ] At least one mirror backend implemented (local-dir is the minimum; s3 preferred)
- [ ] execute-bead finalization triggers an async mirror upload that does not block bead completion
- [ ] mirror-index.jsonl appended with attempt_id, bead_id, mirror_uri, bytes, timestamp
- [ ] Mirror failures logged but do not fail the bead
- [ ] `ddx agent executions fetch <attempt_id>` retrieves a mirrored bundle back to local disk
- [ ] Path template supports {project}, {attempt_id}, {date}, {bead_id}
- [ ] Default config includes the full bundle (embedded included)
- [ ] Integration test: local-dir mirror roundtrip (run execute-bead stub, assert bundle mirrored, fetch it back, bytes match)
- [ ] Docs: update TD-010 (or add TD-011) with the mirror design and operator runbook","claimed-at":"2026-04-18T06:17:17Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"1833233","closing_commit_sha":"d810ddff35013426b0e3ec92f4e1ad1fcb45c777","created_at":"2026-04-15T18:59:41.811906799Z","description":"Execution bundles under .ddx/executions/<attempt>/ are the primary forensic record of what DDx automation did, but the most valuable part — the per-iteration agent trace at embedded/agent-*.jsonl — cannot reasonably be checked in. Individual traces can exceed 50 MB; a busy automation day produces hundreds of MB. Axon hit this on 2026-04-15 when GitHub warned about a 50.18 MB embedded trace file, and embedded/ is now gitignored per-repo.

We still want that level of detail for *every* run, for post-mortems, routing/model analysis, and training-data curation. We need a configurable out-of-band mirror.

## Proposed shape

1. **Archive target** configured via `.ddx/config.yaml` (and/or env var):
   - `executions.mirror.kind: local | s3 | gcs | http`
   - `executions.mirror.path: s3://bucket/ddx-executions/{project}/{attempt_id}/` (template with `{project}`, `{attempt_id}`, `{date}`, `{bead_id}`)
   - `executions.mirror.include: [manifest, prompt, result, usage, checks, embedded]` (allow excluding embedded for bandwidth-sensitive setups, but default = everything)
   - `executions.mirror.async: true` (upload in background after execute-bead returns so it never blocks the hot path)

2. **When to mirror**: at execute-bead finalization, after result.json is written. On failure, retry async with bounded attempts; log failures to `.ddx/agent-logs/mirror.log` but do not fail the bead.

3. **Index**: each mirrored bundle gets a pointer row appended to a local `.ddx/executions/mirror-index.jsonl` (attempt_id, bead_id, mirror_uri, uploaded_at, byte_size). This gives analysts a quick `jq` lookup without walking the remote store.

4. **Retrieval**: `ddx agent executions fetch <attempt_id>` pulls a bundle back to .ddx/executions/<attempt_id>/ for local inspection.

5. **GC**: optional local retention policy (`executions.retain_days`) that prunes old local bundles while the mirror keeps the full history.

## Non-goals

- Not a full audit trail service — just a mirror of DDx's existing bundles.
- Not a replacement for git-tracked manifest/prompt/result/usage summaries — those stay checked in.
- Not a distributed log aggregator — one-shot copy per bundle, no streaming.

## Why now

Axon's ADR-018 session produced ~85 MB of execution artifacts in a single day; only 1.5 MB of that is useful as checked-in summary. Without a mirror, every project using DDx execute-bead faces the same tension: either gitignore the traces and lose the forensic data, or commit them and bloat the repo forever.","events":[{"actor":"ddx","body":"{"resolved_provider":"claude","resolved_model":"qwen3.6-35b-a3b","fallback_chain":[]}","created_at":"2026-04-17T18:23:42.944017231Z","kind":"routing","source":"ddx agent execute-bead","summary":"provider=claude model=qwen3.6-35b-a3b"},{"actor":"ddx","body":"execution_failed

./.agents/skills/.ddx/beads.jsonl:221:{"acceptance":"**User story:** As an operator looking at the DDx web UI, I want to understand at a glance what Workers are vs Sessions, and I want to stop a misbehaving worker or start a new queue drain without dropping to a terminal, so the UI is a real operator surface instead of a read-only status page.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. **Copy + IA.** Workers page header includes a one-sentence description distinguishing it from Sessions. Sessions page likewise. Each links to the other. Playwright asserts both descriptions are present and the cross-links navigate.

2. **Worker → Sessions navigation.** On worker detail, a "Sessions" section lists sessions produced by that worker (join on `session.workerId`). Empty state reads "No sessions recorded yet." If session records do not yet carry a worker ID, that gap is filled as part of this bead and backfilled for existing sessions in the current run (no historical backfill required).

3. **Session → Worker backlink.** Expanded session row shows worker ID as a clickable link when present.

4. **Stop worker.** Each row with `state == 'running'` shows a Stop button. Clicking prompts for confirm, then fires a GraphQL mutation that stops the worker. UI updates within 2s via the existing subscription/poll path. Integration test asserts: button visibility is gated on state, confirm dialog is required, and mutation invokes the same code path as `ddx agent workers stop`.

5. **Start worker.** A "Start worker" button in the Workers page header opens a form (harness/profile, effort, optional label filter). Submitting fires a `startWorker` mutation. On success the new worker appears in the list within 2s. Form validation prevents empty submissions. The code path reuses `ddx work` / execute-loop internals — not a separate implementation.

6. **Audit trail.** Every lifecycle action (start, stop, cancel) is recorded as a timestamped event visible on the worker detail page, including the actor identifier the server has for the requester.

7. **No regressions.** Existing live log streaming, recent events, and phase overrides continue to work. Playwright smoke of the worker detail page passes.

8. **Playwright e2e.**
   - Navigates to Workers page, asserts header description.
   - Clicks "Start worker", fills form, submits, asserts new row appears.
   - Clicks Stop on that row, confirms, asserts row state transitions to `stopped`.
   - Clicks into the stopped worker, asserts the Sessions section renders (or the empty state).
   - Asserts the "Sessions" link in the worker page header navigates to the Sessions page.

9. **Design review sign-off.** Before implementation begins, a short design note (one page or less) in `docs/helix/02-design/` captures the Workers-vs-Sessions mental model, the chosen controls, and any CLI primitives that need to exist. Implementation PR links to that note.","closing_commit_sha":"1811cb188c77a7a20b042f8d08d579228d9368f4","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:18:04.067488813Z","description":"## Observed

`/nodes/.../projects/.../workers` renders a table of workers but has no buttons, no controls, and no explanation of how it differs from `/sessions`. The layout (`cli/internal/server/frontend/src/routes/nodes/[nodeId]/projects/[projectId]/workers/+layout.svelte`) shows ID / Kind / State / Current Bead / Attempts — read-only. Clicking a row drills into live-streaming logs and recent events, but offers no actions there either.

This raised a fair question: **what is this screen for, and how is it different from Sessions?** The answer isn't in the UI.

## Ground truth (from the CLI)

- **Worker** = a persistent execute-loop process draining the bead queue (`ddx work` / `ddx agent execute-loop`). Has a lifecycle: running / idle / stopped / error. Has a *current bead* and cumulative attempts/successes/failures. Can be stopped via `ddx agent workers stop`. Long-lived.
- **Session** = a historical record of one agent invocation (harness, model, bead, tokens, cost, outcome, start/end). Immutable after completion. One worker produces many sessions over its lifetime.

So: Workers are *processes you operate*. Sessions are *events you review*. The current UI exposes both as flat tables and lets the reader guess which is which.

## Gaps

1. **No disambiguation.** No page-level copy, no link between the two, no worker→sessions navigation ("show sessions produced by this worker"), no session→worker backlink.
2. **No lifecycle controls on Workers.** The CLI has `ddx agent workers stop` and `ddx work` (start). The UI has neither button. No stop, start, restart, or "cancel current bead" affordance. The State column is a spectator sport.
3. **No worker start UX.** There is no way to start a queue drain from the UI — users must drop to a terminal.
4. **Unclear empty state.** "No workers found." does not say whether that is normal (nothing running) or broken (server can't see workers). No hint about how to start one.
5. **No worker details beyond logs.** Worker detail page shows live phase + log tail + tool calls but not: worker config (project path, profile/harness, started-at, last-heartbeat), resource usage, or the list of sessions this worker has produced.

## Proposed direction

Split into two deliverables — do them together if we're touching this screen anyway, but they are independently landable:

### A. Information architecture (copy + navigation)

- Add a short header description to each page: "Workers drain the bead queue. Sessions are the history of what they ran."
- Mutual links: Workers page links to "Recent sessions →", Sessions page links to "Workers →".
- On worker detail: a "Sessions" tab or section listing sessions where `session.workerId == this.id` (requires session records to carry workerID; check whether they do).
- On session detail (expandable row already exists): if the session has a workerID, surface it as a link back to the worker.

### B. Lifecycle controls

- **Stop** button per running worker row → calls a GraphQL mutation that invokes the equivalent of `ddx agent workers stop <id>`. Confirms before acting.
- **Start worker** button in the page header → opens a small form (project, profile/harness, effort, optional bead filter) and calls a `startWorker` mutation that shells out to `ddx work` in the background. The form should default to sane values and be submittable in one click for the common case.
- **Cancel current bead** action on the worker detail page — signals the worker to abandon the in-flight bead without killing the loop. (Only if CLI primitive exists; otherwise descope and note in the bead.)
- All mutations must be idempotent and log an audit record viewable in the UI (who did it, when). Reuse existing session-event plumbing if possible.

### Out of scope

- Rewriting execute-loop semantics.
- Cross-project worker orchestration.
- Auth / permissions model for who can stop workers (assume single-operator for now; file a follow-up if multi-operator becomes real).","events":[{"actor":"ddx","body":"{"resolved_provider":"omlx-vidar-1235","resolved_model":"qwen/qwen3.6-35b-a3b","fallback_chain":[]}","created_at":"2026-04-22T21:00:24.797884238Z","kind":"routing","source":"ddx agent execute-bead","summary":"provider=omlx-vidar-1235 model=qwen/qwen3.6-35b-a3b"},{"actor":"ddx","body":"tier=cheap harness=agent model=qwen/qwen3.6-35b-a3b probe=ok

     form (e.g. copy wording, test assertion tightness), cite the file:line

     targeted edit rather than a re-do.
  3. If a "cancel current bead without killing the loop" control is now
     wanted, that needs a new CLI/runtime primitive first — file as its
     own bead, since the design note explicitly descoped it.

Why no commit
-------------
Per execute-bead instructions: "a well-justified no_changes is better than
a bad commit." The bead requirements are met; there is nothing to add.
Recommitting the same code under a new bead attempt would churn git
history without changing the acceptance state. The correct action is to
let the bead close on its existing evidence (commit b22b81cb) and, if the
reviewer still sees gaps, re-open with a file:line-specific finding.

./.agents/skills/.ddx/beads.jsonl:229:{"acceptance":"**User story:** As a developer on the Efficacy page, I understand what Compare is for the moment I see it, and I can either select rows I already care about and compare them, or I can ignore the feature entirely if it's not for me.

**Acceptance criteria (assume outcome (2) — "redesign in place"; rewrite these if (1) or (3) is chosen):**

1. **Decision note.** One-pager in `docs/helix/02-design/` records the chosen outcome (relocate / redesign / remove), the evidence behind it (usage grep, dependency scan), and the scope of remaining work. Implementation PR links to it.

2. **Row-selection entry point.** Efficacy table rows gain a leading checkbox column. Selecting ≥2 rows enables a "Compare selected" button in the page header. With 0 or 1 rows selected the button is disabled (not hidden) with a tooltip explaining why.

3. **Pre-seeded comparison.** Clicking "Compare selected" launches the existing comparison flow with arms pre-populated from the selected rows' `(harness, provider, model)` tuples. The prompt field is still required (comparisons need a prompt) but is the only field the user has to fill in — the arm list is done.

4. **Removed from cold-start.** The standalone "Compare" button on the Efficacy page (the one that creates an empty comparison) is removed. New comparisons originate from a row selection. If users legitimately want a from-scratch comparison, that path lives on a separate `/experiments` page (out of scope for this bead unless the design note calls for it).

5. **Empty-state copy.** When the Efficacy table is empty, the Compare affordance is hidden entirely (no point selecting zero rows).

6. **Playwright e2e.**
   - Seeds the fixture with ≥3 efficacy rows.
   - Asserts the Compare button is disabled with 0/1 selections and enabled with 2+.
   - Selects 2 rows, clicks Compare, asserts the comparison form opens with arms pre-populated from those rows and only the prompt field is empty.

7. **No regression to efficacy perf.** All perf targets from `ddx-0a33bc5f` still hold. Adding a checkbox column does not change the payload the resolver returns.","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:47:38.842277927Z","description":"## Observed

The Efficacy page has a "Compare" button that opens a flow for adding "arms" and writing a prompt. User feedback: they're not sure what to do with it. It does not match their mental model of what efficacy is (a session aggregation grouped by provider / harness / model).

## Ground truth

- The backing feature is `Query.comparisons` and the comparison record set (`cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_feat008.go:211-226`). It's an A/B-style "run the same prompt across N arms and compare results" tool. That is a real capability — just not what a rollup/efficacy dashboard is for.
- It's colocated with Efficacy today probably because both involve harness/model performance. The colocation is creating confusion: the Efficacy page promises "how are things doing" and the Compare button promises "design an experiment" — different audiences, different tasks.

## Decision required

Before implementation, decide one of three:

1. **Relocate.** Move Compare to its own top-level surface (e.g., `/experiments` or `/compare`) and cleanly separate "how did things go" (efficacy) from "design an A/B" (compare). Efficacy page loses the Compare button entirely.
2. **Redesign in place.** Keep it on the Efficacy page but recast as "Compare these rows" — the user selects 2+ efficacy rows and launches a comparison pre-seeded from them. Removes the cold-start prompt-entry UX; connects directly to the rollup the user is already looking at.
3. **Remove.** If comparisons are not being used by real workflows, drop the feature and the associated UI/resolver. Least code, least surface area.

The user did not mandate a specific choice — they flagged the confusion and the mental model. Option 2 aligns best with their stated mental model (efficacy = session aggregation); option 1 is the cleanest separation of concerns; option 3 is viable only if usage data supports it.

## Proposed direction

Run the design decision as a short spike (≤ half a day):

- Check whether `comparisons` is referenced by anything beyond this UI page: CLI, docs, specs, other server endpoints. Deletion cost is a function of that.
- Usage check: grep agent-logs + bead evidence for any `comparison_id` or `compare` correlation over the last 30 days. Is anyone actually running comparisons?
- Write a one-page decision note in `docs/helix/02-design/` recording the choice, the evidence that supports it, and the scope of the implementation work.

This bead's remaining acceptance criteria are written assuming the outcome is (2) "redesign in place" — the most-likely-to-be-built option. They should be rewritten when the decision is made if the outcome is (1) or (3).

## Out of scope

- Anything the Efficacy perf/source rework (`ddx-0a33bc5f`) covers.
- Multi-run orchestration changes to how comparisons execute.","events":[{"actor":"ddx","body":"{"resolved_provider":"omlx-vidar-1235","resolved_model":"qwen/qwen3.6-35b-a3b","fallback_chain":[]}","created_at":"2026-04-22T21:01:57.817147436Z","kind":"routing","source":"ddx agent execute-bead","summary":"provider=omlx-vidar-1235 model=qwen/qwen3.6-35b-a3b"},{"actor":"ddx","body":"tier=cheap harness=agent model=qwen/qwen3.6-35b-a3b probe=ok

./.agents/skills/.ddx/beads.jsonl:241:{"acceptance":"**User story:** As a developer reviewing what my agents have done, I open the Executions view, filter to a specific bead or verdict or harness, click into one, and see the prompt it received, the verdict it produced, the session it corresponds to (with cost + tokens), and every tool call it made — all without leaving the browser.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. **Sidebar entry.** "Executions" added to the project sidebar between "Sessions" and "Personas" (or wherever fits the existing visual order — design call; captured in the PR description). Active-highlight works.

2. **List route.** `/executions` renders a paginated, filterable table with columns: timestamp, bead (id + title as link), harness/model, verdict (color-coded pass/block/error/other), duration, cost. Default sort: newest first. Filters: verdict, harness, bead, since/until date, free-text search over prompt first ~200 chars.

3. **Detail route.** `/executions/<id>` shows five tabs as described (Manifest, Prompt, Result, Session, Tool calls). Tab switching doesn't re-fetch already-loaded data.

4. **Tool calls pane.** Virtualized list. First 50 calls load with the page; subsequent pages load on scroll. Each entry shows tool name + collapsed input/output, expandable on click. Links to the raw agent-log file for operators who want to diff.

5. **GraphQL completeness.** `executions`, `execution`, `executionToolCalls` resolvers exist and pass feat-008-style integration tests. Schema documented in `schema.graphql`.

6. **Cross-links present.**
   - Bead detail renders an "Executions" section listing linked executions with verdict + timestamp, each clicking into the detail route.
   - Session detail shows "Execution" link when the session has one.
   - Commits page shows execution link when commit sha == execution.result_rev.

7. **Fixture + perf.**
   - Seeded fixture: ≥1,000 executions, ≥100 with seeded tool-call streams (≥50 calls each).
   - List view on this fixture: p95 ≤ 200ms HTTP.
   - Detail view (no tool calls): p95 ≤ 150ms HTTP.
   - Tool-call page 1 (first 50 entries): p95 ≤ 300ms HTTP.
   - If any target cannot be met in-place, raise DB-substrate decision with user before switching.

8. **Playwright e2e.**
   - Seeds a project with 3 executions across 2 beads with mixed verdicts.
   - Navigates to `/executions`, asserts the table renders 3 rows and filter-by-verdict narrows correctly.
   - Clicks into one execution, asserts all 5 tabs exist and the Prompt tab shows the seeded prompt.
   - Opens the Tool calls tab, expands an entry, asserts input/output rendering.
   - Clicks the linked bead from the detail, asserts navigation to bead detail and that the Executions section on that bead lists this execution.

9. **Depends on.**
   - Soft dependency on `ddx-2ceb02fa` for the Session tab's cost/token numbers. If not yet landed, render "not available" for those fields and wire them in after.
   - Adjacent: `ddx-6904a90b` (billing mode) — Session tab's cost shows the correct bucket once that lands.","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:55:34.427486388Z","description":"## Observed / Motivation

`.ddx/executions/<ts>-<hash>/{manifest.json, prompt.md, result.json}` bundles are already being produced per `ddx agent execute-bead` attempt — 469 of them on the current repo. They contain the canonical record of what an agent was asked to do and what verdict the attempt produced. **Nothing in the web UI surfaces them today.** A user who wants to ask "why did this bead end up in this state?" has to drop to the filesystem.

Sessions (`sessions.jsonl` / sharded index per `ddx-2ceb02fa`) carry the harness/cost/token telemetry but not the verdict and not the bead linkage. Agent log streams (`agent-*.jsonl`) carry the tool-call trace but are scattered across hundreds of files. **Executions are the natural join** — they connect a bead, a session, a prompt, a verdict, and a tool-call stream.

## Ground truth

Per-execution on disk:

```
.ddx/executions/<ts>-<hash>/
  manifest.json      { harness, model, base_rev, result_rev, verdict, bead_id, execution_dir }
  prompt.md          the prompt that was sent
  result.json        { verdict, rationale, ... }
```

Relations:
- `manifest.bead_id` → bead
- a session id is recorded in bead events (`kind:cost`, `kind:routing`) and in the agent-log stream; both can map back to an execution via correlation metadata
- tool calls live in `.ddx/agent-logs/agent-*.jsonl` (and `agent-s-*.jsonl`), keyed by session id

## Scope

### Part 1 — GraphQL surface

- `executions(projectId, first, after, beadId?, since?, until?, verdict?, harness?)` — paginated list of execution bundles.
- `execution(id)` — one bundle, fully materialized: manifest fields, prompt markdown, result JSON, linked bead (id + title), linked session (id + harness + model + cost + token totals), tool-call stream pointer.
- `executionToolCalls(id, first, after)` — paginated tool-call stream for the execution, read lazily from the agent-log file. Matches the event shape used by the workers detail page.

Executions source of truth: directory scan of `.ddx/executions/` filtered/paginated at the resolver. Shard discipline: follow the sessions-index monthly shard pattern (`ddx-2ceb02fa`) if listing all executions grows beyond tens of thousands — per standing directive `ddx-9ce6842a`, raise the storage decision with the user before switching substrate.

### Part 2 — Sidebar + routes

- Add "Executions" to the project sidebar (`NavShell.svelte`).
- Routes:
  - `/nodes/<n>/projects/<p>/executions` — list view with filters (verdict, harness, bead, date range, free-text over prompt). Table columns: timestamp, bead, harness/model, verdict (pass/block/error), duration, cost.
  - `/nodes/<n>/projects/<p>/executions/<id>` — detail view with five tabs: **Manifest**, **Prompt** (rendered markdown), **Result** (rationale + raw JSON toggle), **Session** (harness, model, cost, token totals, link to `/sessions/<id>`), **Tool calls** (virtualized lazy-scroll list of tool_use + tool_result pairs with expandable payloads).

### Part 3 — Cross-links (make the graph real)

- **Bead detail:** new "Executions" section listing all executions for this bead with verdict + timestamp + link. Already have data: manifest.bead_id.
- **Session detail:** new "Execution" link pointing to the execution this session ran for, when the session has a linked execution.
- **Commits page:** already shows commits; if a commit was produced by an execution (result_rev == commit sha), surface the execution link.
- **Graph/Docs pages:** out of scope for this bead.

### Part 4 — Perf

- Fixture: ≥1,000 executions, per standing directive `ddx-9ce6842a`. Tool-call streams seeded for 10% of them.
- Targets: list view p95 ≤ 200ms HTTP, detail view without tool calls p95 ≤ 150ms HTTP, tool-call page-1 (first 50) p95 ≤ 300ms HTTP.
- If these can't be met with filesystem scan + in-memory filter, raise DB-substrate decision with user per standing directive.

## Out of scope

- Writing new execution bundles (that's already happening).
- Executions from other projects/nodes (keep per-project for v1).
- Tool-call payload redaction / privacy. File a follow-up if relevant.
- `.claude/worktrees/` worktree lifecycle tracking — covered by companion bead per user's split request.","events":[{"actor":"ddx","body":"{"resolved_provider":"omlx-vidar-1235","resolved_model":"qwen/qwen3.6-35b-a3b","fallback_chain":[]}","created_at":"2026-04-22T21:01:23.105870644Z","kind":"routing","source":"ddx agent execute-bead","summary":"provider=omlx-vidar-1235 model=qwen/qwen3.6-35b-a3b"},{"actor":"ddx","body":"tier=cheap harness=agent model=qwen/qwen3.6-35b-a3b probe=ok

./.agents/skills/.ddx/beads.jsonl:265:{"acceptance":"**User story:** As a developer on the plugins page, I want clicking Update or Install to give me continuous feedback until the operation completes, and I want the card to reflect the new installed state automatically — without a page reload.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. **Per-card in-flight tracking.** Clicking Update/Install records the workerId under the plugin's name in a `Map<string, string>`. The same plugin cannot be dispatched again while it is in flight (button disabled; tooltip shows the worker link). A different plugin can be dispatched concurrently and tracks its own worker.

2. **Worker-driven refresh.** For each in-flight plugin, the page subscribes to that worker's progress via the existing `subscribeWorkerProgress` channel. On a terminal event, the page refetches the plugins list and clears that plugin's entry from the in-flight map. Within 2s of worker terminal event, the card reflects the new status (e.g., `update-available` → `installed`, new `installedVersion`).

3. **Fallback polling.** If no terminal event arrives within 30s of dispatch, the page begins polling `plugins { name status installedVersion }` every 2s until the card's status or installedVersion changes, then stops. Integration test simulates a stream dropout and asserts the card eventually refreshes via the poll path.

4. **In-flight visual state.** While a card is in flight, its status badge is replaced by a spinner + "Updating…" or "Installing…" text. The action button is disabled with a tooltip pointing to the worker.

5. **Failure surfacing.** If the worker terminates in a failure phase, the card shows an error badge + tooltip "Update failed — view worker" linking to the worker page. The card's status/version remains whatever it was before dispatch (no false success). The global `dispatchError` stays for synchronous mutation errors only.

6. **Concurrent dispatch.** Dispatching Update on two different cards at once shows two active worker links (a row or small stack in the header instead of the single slot), and each card tracks its own state. Playwright asserts.

7. **No extra refetch overhead.** Refetch is scoped to the plugins list, not the full page data. Manual verification is enough; no specific perf test required.

8. **Playwright e2e.**
   - Seeds a fixture with one outdated plugin.
   - Clicks Update.
   - Asserts the card enters the in-flight visual state and the action button is disabled.
   - Simulates the worker completing successfully.
   - Asserts within 2s the card shows `installed` and a newer `installedVersion`.
   - Runs a second scenario where the simulated worker fails; asserts the failure badge and that status stays `update-available`.

9. **No regressions.** Install-from-registry flow (the ConfirmDialog path) adopts the same in-flight tracking; existing Playwright install flow still passes.","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:32:29.080474392Z","description":"## Observed

On `/nodes/.../projects/.../plugins`, clicking Update on the outdated `helix` card:

1. Fires the mutation — works.
2. Shows a link to the dispatched worker — helpful.
3. Worker completes and the plugin is actually updated on disk — verified.
4. **Card still shows `update-available`.** The UI never closes the loop.

The user has no in-UI signal that the update succeeded. They must reload the page (or navigate away and back) to see the new state. Worse, nothing prevents them from clicking the button again during the gap — firing a second update.

## Root cause

`cli/internal/server/frontend/src/routes/nodes/[nodeId]/projects/[projectId]/plugins/+page.svelte:74-89` —

```js
async function dispatchPlugin(name, action, scope = 'project') {
  const result = await client.request(PLUGIN_DISPATCH_MUTATION, { name, action, scope });
  workerId = result.pluginDispatch.id;
}
```

That is the entire post-dispatch behavior. `data.plugins` is the snapshot from `+page.ts` at load time. There is no:

- subscription to the dispatched worker's progress,
- polling of the plugin list,
- refetch on worker terminal event,
- optimistic card state while the worker runs,
- per-card in-flight tracking (only a single `workerId: string | null` at module scope, which means dispatching a second plugin overwrites the first worker link).

The workers layout already has `subscribeWorkerProgress(workerID, cb)` (`cli/internal/server/frontend/src/routes/nodes/[nodeId]/projects/[projectId]/workers/+layout.svelte:22-30`) — the plugins page just doesn't use it.

## Proposed direction

Two changes, smallest first:

### Fix A — Close the loop on the dispatched worker.

- Per-card in-flight tracking: replace the singleton `workerId` with a `Map<pluginName, workerId>`. Each card shows its own worker link + in-flight state; clicking Install/Update is disabled while that plugin is in flight.
- Subscribe to `subscribeWorkerProgress` for each in-flight plugin. On a terminal phase event (`completed`, `failed`, etc. — whatever the existing worker event vocabulary uses), refetch the plugins list and clear that plugin's in-flight entry.
- If the existing worker-progress stream doesn't emit a terminal event with enough detail, fall back to polling `plugins { name status installedVersion }` every 2s while any card is in flight, stopping when none are.

### Fix B — Optimistic card state (optional polish).

- While a card is in-flight, show "Updating…" / "Installing…" with a spinner in place of the status badge, and disable the action button. This is orthogonal to Fix A and can ship with it.

### Fix C — Persistent failure surfacing.

- Currently `dispatchError` is a single string. If the worker terminates with a failure phase, surface that per-card (error badge + tooltip with detail link to the worker page) rather than relying on `dispatchError` which only covers the synchronous mutation error.

## Out of scope

- Designing new worker terminal events. Use whatever the workers layout already consumes.
- Cancelling an in-flight plugin update — follow-up if needed.
- Bulk update ("update all outdated"). ./.agents/skills/.ddx/beads.jsonl:287:{"acceptance":"**User story:** As an operator using a browser — on a machine without a terminal, or a phone, or a laptop away from my dev box — I can open my project's home page, type a prompt into a chat pane, and see the DDx agent work on it in real time. Tool calls stream as they happen, costs accrue visibly, and the conversation persists so I can come back to it.

**Acceptance criteria (written for Option A; rewrite if design note chooses B/C/D):**

1. **Design note landed.** One-pager in `docs/helix/02-design/` records the chosen option, the answers to the six subsidiary questions, and the message/thread schema. Implementation PR links to it.

2. **Thread model.** Chat threads persist under `.ddx/chat/<thread-id>/{manifest.json,messages.jsonl}`. One thread per project per operator by default; operators can create named threads. Messages.jsonl is append-only, one JSON object per message (user or assistant), with `sessionId` linking each assistant turn back to an entry in the sessions index (`ddx-2ceb02fa`).

3. **GraphQL surface.** New resolvers:
   - Query: `chatThreads(projectId)`, `chatThread(id)`, `chatMessages(threadId, since?)`.
   - Mutation: `chatThreadCreate(projectId, title?)`, `chatSend(threadId, message, harness?, profile?)` → kicks off a session via `service.Execute`.
   - Subscription: `chatStream(threadId)` → emits assistant message chunks + tool call events (same event vocabulary as `workers.recentEvents`).

4. **Home page UI.** Project home page gains a chat pane below the actions section. Pane shows thread list, active thread messages, input box, harness/profile picker. Tool calls render inline within assistant messages. Streaming updates as chunks arrive.

5. **Session linkage.** Each assistant turn creates one session in the sessions index. Clicking an assistant message shows the session id and a link to the session detail (`/sessions/<id>`). Chat thread header shows cumulative session count + total cost for the thread.

6. **Concurrency.** Two browsers open on the same thread see each other's messages within 2s (subscription-driven). Two operators on different threads in the same project do not interfere.

7. **Interrupt.** The in-flight assistant turn can be cancelled via a Stop button; server sends the harness the equivalent of `ddx agent workers stop <id>` for the in-flight session. The thread records a cancelled turn, does not try to auto-resume.

8. **Cost guardrail (soft).** If the cumulative thread cost passes a configurable ceiling (`.ddx/config.yaml: chat.cost_ceiling_usd`, default unset), the UI warns but does not block. Hard block is a follow-up.

9. **Auth posture.**
   - Per design note. If the chosen posture is "single-operator localhost / tsnet only for v1," the chat routes are served unauthenticated behind tsnet and the design note records the gap to close before broader deployment.
   - A small middleware gate is in place even for v1 so adding auth later is schema-local, not route-local.

10. **Playwright e2e.**
    - Seeds a project with zero chat threads.
    - Navigates to project home, asserts the chat pane renders with "Start a new thread" affordance.
    - Starts a thread, sends a short prompt, simulates a streamed session response with one tool call, asserts chunks appear in order and the session id link is present.
    - Opens a second browser context on the same thread, asserts both see new messages within 2s.
    - Cancels an in-flight turn, asserts the cancelled state is persisted.

11. **Depends on.**
    - `ddx-2513f08c` (home page reachable from sidebar) — hard dependency.
    - `ddx-2ceb02fa` (sessions index) — hard dependency for the session linkage + cost rollup.

12. **Explicitly deferred.** PTY integration (Option B / pop-out terminal from Option C) is its own bead, filed only if the Option A rollout surfaces real interactive-steering needs.","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:51:38.374211227Z","description":"## Observed / Motivation

User wants to "lift work out of the TUI entirely and allow non-tmux users to work remotely." Today the only way to prompt the DDx agent stack interactively is a terminal session. The web UI is read-mostly: dispatch three actions (drain/align/checks) and review results. There is no surface that lets an operator say "go do X" against the project in natural language from a browser.

Related UX: the project home page already exists (`/nodes/.../projects/<id>/`), renders queue summary + actions, and is the natural host for a chat affordance. First the home page needs to be reachable from the sidebar (`ddx-2513f08c`) — this bead depends on that work.

## The tension

The DDx harness abstraction (`cli/internal/agent/service_run.go`, `service.Execute`) is designed for **non-interactive** use: prompt in, structured result out, optional streaming. This is the right shape for queue-draining, quorum, evaluation, and automation. It is **not** the shape of a TUI conversation with tab completion, mid-run steering, and back-channel signals.

An operator-facing chat can land on either side of that line. The bead records all four options so the design phase can pick one deliberately.

## Options (design phase decides, recommended path noted)

### Option A — Chat over the harness abstraction (RECOMMENDED starting point)

- Chat UI posts a prompt → server dispatches a session via `service.Execute` (or a wrapper) → streams tool calls + response chunks back over a GraphQL subscription.
- Message history is persisted as a thread; each user turn = one new session. Responses reuse the existing `recentEvents` stream shape already used on the workers detail page.
- Works with every harness today because the harness abstraction is what it is. claude, codex, agent (embedded) all participate.
- **Trade-off:** no mid-run steering, no tab completion, no live back-and-forth inside one agent invocation.
- Matches the user's stated target audience (non-TUI users who want remote browser access). Those users aren't trying to steer a TUI — they're trying to leave it behind.

### Option B — PTY gateway per harness

- Server owns a PTY pool. Each chat thread attaches to one harness process (claude/codex/etc.) over a WebSocket; browser renders xterm.js.
- Full interactivity, including TUI steering.
- **Trade-off:** PTY lifecycle, auth, resource limits, tab-close-loses-session semantics, multi-user access control — all real. The harness abstraction is bypassed for this mode, which creates a second execution path to maintain.

### Option C — Hybrid (chat-first + pop-out terminal)

- Option A is the primary surface. When a chat reaches a point that needs interactive steering, a "Open terminal" escape hatch attaches a PTY to the current harness session.
- **Trade-off:** more surface area, but honest about both use cases. Most sessions stay on the cheap path.

### Option D — Web-tmux / ttyd wrapper

- Embed the existing terminal tooling directly: ttyd or xterm.js over a tmux socket owned by the server.
- **Trade-off:** fastest to ship, zero harness work, but the UX is a terminal in a browser — not "chat." Requires tmux. Doesn't serve the stated "non-tmux users" audience.

### Recommendation

Start with **A**. If the design note documents real interactive-steering needs we can't paper over, the right follow-up is **C** (A + PTY escape hatch), not B (PTY-only).

## Scope of this bead

- Design note in `docs/helix/02-design/` locking the option, the message schema, persistence model, and auth posture. Implementation PR links to it.
- First implementation pass: Option A.
- Home page gains a chat pane as its primary interaction surface, alongside the existing queue summary + actions (which stay — they're quick-dispatch affordances, not replaced by chat).

## Subsidiary questions the design note must answer

1. **Message persistence.** Are chat threads per-project or per-user? Stored where (`.ddx/chat/<thread-id>/` or elsewhere)? Is history shared across operators on the same project, or isolated per operator identity?
2. **Harness selection.** Does the chat pick a harness per-thread (picker in the UI) or per-message (re-route each turn)? Default to profile-based selection?
3. **Cost accountability.** Every chat message spawns a session. Sessions are already tracked in the sessions index (`ddx-2ceb02fa`). The chat thread should surface cumulative cost to the operator so they don't blow budget unknowingly.
4. **Tool execution scope.** Chat prompts will trigger agent tool calls that read/write the project tree. Is the chat session bounded to the same permissions as `ddx agent run`? Does it run in the project worktree, a worktree per thread, a scratch dir? Default safe answer: same worktree as `ddx agent run` (user trusts ddx running anyway), but design note records it.
5. **Concurrency.** What happens if two operators have the chat open on the same project and both send messages? Default: independent threads, each with its own session — no shared state.
6. **Auth.** Today the server assumes single-operator localhost. Remote use implies some identity concept. Design note picks a posture: delegate to reverse proxy, ship a simple token, or punt and require tsnet + single-operator for v1.

## Out of scope

- Full PTY implementation (Option B). Follow-up bead if A+hybrid doesn't suffice.
- Cross-project chat.
- Voice input.","feature":"FEAT-008","id":"ddx-88e91fdb","issue_type":"epic","labels":["feat-008","feat-006","chat","remote-ops","design"],"priority":2,"status":"open","title":"Add chat interface to project home page: prompt ddx remotely without the TUI","updated_at":"2026-04-22T20:51:38.374211227Z"}

Local --local workers are detected via manifest scan of .ddx/.execute-bead-wt-* directories. --json flag emits raw JSON. --watch re-renders every 2s. A worker executing bead axon-abc123 shows that bead ID and its title (truncated). An idle system (no active workers) prints 'no active workers' rather than an empty table. No dependency on worktrunk or any external binary beyond git.","claimed-at":"2026-04-14T07:31:03Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"1412283","closing_commit_sha":"3f16c49fc95957923d3f45fc2a93ec647ba859b4","created_at":"2026-04-13T20:34:44.901809653Z","description":"There is no CLI command to see which workers are running and which bead each is executing. The WorkerManager in cli/internal/server/workers.go already tracks this (WorkerRecord.CurrentBead at line 51, WorkerRecord.State, WorkerRecord.Harness, WorkerRecord.Model, WorkerRecord.Attempts/Successes/Failures) but it is only accessible through the server API. Local --local workers are not tracked by the server at all.

This bead adds ddx agent workers as a top-level CLI subcommand. It must not depend on worktrunk (wt) or any external tool.

## Required changes

### 1. New command: ddx agent workers (cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go)
Register alongside the existing ddx agent log command. Flags:
  --json         emit raw JSON array
  --watch        re-render every 2s (like watch -n2) until Ctrl-C
  --project      filter to a specific project root (default: CWD project)

### 2. Data sources (two paths)
A. Server-submitted workers: GET http://127.0.0.1:8080/api/agent/workers — deserialize []WorkerRecord from the server. Already implemented in cli/internal/server/workers.go List() / HTTP handler.
B. Local --local workers: the server does not track these. Detect them by:
   1. Reading .ddx/workers/*/status.json files in the project root (server writes these even for local workers when they self-register — confirm in workers.go; if not, fall back to scanning active execute-bead worktrees)
   2. Scanning .ddx/.execute-bead-wt-* directories (from ExecuteBeadWtPrefix constant in cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go line 403) and reading the manifest.json inside each to extract bead_id, attempt_id, harness, model, started_at

### 3. Local worker detection via manifest scan
For each directory matching .ddx/.execute-bead-wt-*:
  - Read .ddx/executions/{attemptID}/manifest.json (path encoded in worktree name suffix)
  - Check if worktree is still active (git worktree list --porcelain)
  - Read execute-loop-heartbeat-at from bead.Extra (requires ddx-7651506a) if available
  - Derive synthetic WorkerRecord from manifest fields

### 4. Table output (default)
Columns: STATE | BEAD | TITLE (truncated 40 chars) | HARNESS | MODEL | ELAPSED | ATTEMPTS
- STATE: running (green), exited (dim), failed (red)
- ELAPSED: human-readable (e.g., 4m32s)
- If current bead title unavailable (local worker pre-manifest), show bead ID

### 5. Merge and deduplicate
When both server and manifest sources are available, deduplicate by worker_id or attempt_id. Server record takes precedence over manifest-derived record for the same worker.

## Key files
- cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go — add newAgentWorkersCommand(), register in newAgentCommand()
- cli/internal/server/workers.go — WorkerRecord struct (lines 31-54), List API
- cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go — ExecuteBeadWtPrefix constant (line 403), manifest shape (lines 726-771)
- cli/internal/agent/types.go — SessionEntry Correlation map (bead_id field)","events":[{"actor":"ddx","body":"success

./.agents/skills/.ddx/beads.jsonl:330:{"acceptance":"**User story:** As a developer looking at the personas UI, I want to understand at a glance what personas are, where they come from (library vs project), and I want to fork or create a project-local persona without leaving the UI. Library content stays read-only so I cannot accidentally mutate shared content; project content is fully editable.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. **Design note + policy decision recorded.** Before implementation, a one-page note in `docs/helix/02-design/` captures D1 (library vs project policy, override rules, file locations) and D2 (scoped UI additions). Implementation PR links to it.

2. **Project-local persona directory.** `.ddx/personas/*.md` is a recognized location. `ddx persona --list` shows both library and project personas with a `source` tag. Override rule: a project persona with the same name as a library persona wins for that project's bindings; unit test covers this precedence.

3. **GraphQL additions.** New mutations: `personaCreate(name, body, projectId) → Persona`, `personaUpdate(name, body, projectId) → Persona`, `personaDelete(name, projectId) → {ok}`. All gated on `source == "project"`; attempting any of them on a library persona returns a typed error. Each mutation persists to `.ddx/personas/<name>.md` under the given project.

4. **CLI parity.** `ddx persona new`, `edit`, `fork`, `delete` exist and share the same underlying operations as the GraphQL mutations (no duplicated logic). Integration test asserts that a `ddx persona new` followed by a GraphQL `personas` query returns the new persona.

5. **UI — explainer copy.** The personas page shows a one-sentence description of what personas are and a second sentence distinguishing library vs project sources. Playwright asserts both sentences are present.

6. **UI — source badge.** Each persona row shows a `library` or `project` badge. Playwright seeds one of each and asserts correct badges.

7. **UI — New / Edit / Delete for project personas.** A "New persona" button opens a form with name + body fields. Project-source rows have Edit and Delete affordances. Library-source rows have neither. Playwright creates a persona, edits it, verifies the edit persisted, then deletes it.

8. **UI — Fork library to project.** Library rows have a "Fork to project" action. Clicking it prompts for a name (default: library name with `-local` suffix on collision), creates `.ddx/personas/<name>.md`, and navigates to the editor. Playwright asserts the forked file exists and opens in the editor.

9. **No regressions.** Existing list/show/bind flows still work. Playwright smoke of the pre-existing binding UX passes.

10. **Empty project-local state.** When a project has zero project-local personas, the UI shows a subtle hint: "No project personas yet. Fork a library persona or create a new one." Playwright asserts.","closing_commit_sha":"69c60d902a41f3a24af908881f4ef14ba1ff3832","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:30:40.66472156Z","description":"## Observed

`/nodes/.../projects/.../personas` is a well-crafted browser + bind UX, but it is read-only: no add, no edit, no delete, no explanation of what the reader is looking at or why they cannot change it. A user looking at it can reasonably ask "what is this for?" and "why can't I change anything?" and the UI answers neither.

## Ground truth

- **What a persona is.** A markdown file with frontmatter under `library/personas/*.md` defining AI personality, expertise, principles, communication style. Shipped defaults: architect, code-reviewer, implementer, specification-enforcer, test-engineer.
- **How it's used.** Projects declare role→persona bindings in `.ddx/config.yaml` via `persona_bindings`. When an agent performs role X, the bound persona's body is injected into its system prompt.
- **What the tool exposes today.**
  - CLI: `ddx persona --list`, `--show <name>`, `--bind <persona> --role <role>`. No create/edit/delete.
  - GraphQL: `personaBind` mutation only (`cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_feat008.go:150`). No `personaCreate/Update/Delete` in schema.
  - UI: list/show/bind parity with CLI.
- **Why it evolved this way.** Personas have been treated as *library content* — authored carefully, version-controlled upstream, pulled into projects via `ddx update` and contributed back via `ddx contribute`. The tool deliberately did not become a persona editor.

## The gap

The library-only model doesn't hold up for real use:

1. **Project-local personas are a real need.** Teams want "our code-reviewer, with our opinions about our codebase." Today they either (a) fork the persona file and push upstream (wrong — it's not generally useful), (b) never customize (bad outcomes), or (c) drop a file into some ad-hoc local path and hope it works.
2. **The UI implies capability it doesn't have.** A list + detail view with no hint about source policy reads as "unfinished feature" rather than "intentional read-only".
3. **Binding alone isn't enough.** Even if editing stays out of scope, users need to understand *which personas are available*, *where they come from* (library vs project-local), and *how to change that*.

## Proposed direction

Two decisions (pick before implementation):

### D1 — Policy: who owns persona content?

Recommend: **both, with a clear boundary.**
- Library personas: `library/personas/*.md` — read-only in UI, contribute upstream via `ddx contribute`.
- Project-local personas: new directory `.ddx/personas/*.md` — fully editable in UI, live with the project, not in the shared library. Override semantics: a project-local persona with the same name as a library persona takes precedence for that project's bindings.

### D2 — Scope of the UI

Minimum viable, given D1:
- **Explainer copy** on the personas page header: "Personas are AI personality templates. Library personas are shared; project personas live with this project. Bind a persona to a role in `.ddx/config.yaml`."
- **Source badge** on each persona row: `library` or `project`.
- **Project-local editor.** For project personas:
  - `New persona` button → form (name, roles/tags, body markdown). Creates `.ddx/personas/<name>.md` with frontmatter.
  - `Edit` on project rows.
  - `Delete` on project rows with confirm.
- **Library rows stay read-only.** Action on a library row: `Fork to project` — copies the library file to `.ddx/personas/<name>.md` (with a default rename suggestion on collision) and opens the editor. No in-place edit, no delete.
- **Bind affordance remains.** Binding UI continues to work for both sources.

## What this bead does NOT decide

- Multi-project persona sharing (e.g., org-level personas between library and project). File a follow-up if it becomes pressing.
- Rich editor affordances beyond a plain markdown textarea with preview. Frontmatter can be a YAML-ish textarea for v1.
- Syncing project personas upstream — that remains `ddx contribute` and is out of scope.
- Any change to how personas are injected into agent prompts (the read path on the server).

## CLI parity

Whatever the UI gains, the CLI gains too. Add:
- `ddx persona new <name>` — creates `.ddx/personas/<name>.md` scaffold.
- `ddx persona edit <name>` — opens `$EDITOR` on the project-local persona (errors on library).
- `ddx persona fork <library-name> [--as <new-name>]` — copies library → project.
- `ddx persona delete <name>` — project-local only.

SUMMARY: No new commit is needed — every AC is already satisfied by the
current HEAD of this worktree. The implementation was landed by earlier
attempts and is present in this attempt's base-rev. The review blocks on
prior attempts were caused by the review tool inspecting only
`result.json`, not the full bead branch; the code itself has been in main
and continues to pass all persona-scoped tests.

WHAT IS ALREADY DONE (walkthrough by AC)

AC#1 — Design note:
  docs/helix/02-design/plan-2026-04-22-persona-lifecycle.md
  Records D1 (library/project policy + override rules + file locations)
  and D2 (scoped UI additions), with a table mapping each area to the
  files that implement it.

AC#2 — Project-local persona directory + precedence:
  .ddx/personas/<name>.md is recognized by:
    - cli/internal/persona/loader.go (NewPersonaLoaderWithDirs,
      resolveProjectPersonasDir, ListPersonas project-first walk)
    - cli/cmd/persona.go personaList/personaShow/personaBind
  Unit coverage: cli/internal/persona/project_personas_test.go
    - TestLoader_ProjectOverridesLibrary (precedence)
    - TestLoader_SourceBadges (source tag)
    - TestProjectPersonaWriter (CRUD + ReadOnlyLibrary refusal)

AC#3 — GraphQL mutations:
  Schema: cli/internal/server/graphql/schema.graphql
    personaCreate/personaUpdate/personaDelete/personaFork + types
    Persona.source, PersonaDeleteResult.
  Resolvers: cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_feat008.go
    PersonaCreate/PersonaUpdate/PersonaDelete/PersonaFork all delegate
    to ProjectPersonaWriter and surface PersonaError via
    personaGraphQLError() as typed errors with a `code` extension.
  Integration: cli/internal/server/graphql/persona_lifecycle_test.go
    TestIntegration_PersonaLifecycle hits all three mutations + fork,
    asserts `persona_is_library_read_only` on library targets, and
    asserts CLI-created personas show up via the personas query.

AC#4 — CLI parity:
  Subcommands implemented in cli/cmd/persona.go
    `ddx persona new <name> [--body]`    → writer.Create
    `ddx persona edit <name> [--body]`   → writer.Update (+ $EDITOR path)
    `ddx persona fork <lib> [--as]`      → writer.Fork
    `ddx persona delete <name>`          → writer.Delete
  Registered in cli/cmd/command_factory_commands.go alongside the
  existing subcommands.
  Tests: cli/cmd/persona_lifecycle_test.go
    TestPersonaCLI_Lifecycle (all 4 subcommands happy/sad paths)
    TestPersonaCLI_GraphQLParity (CLI-created persona visible to the
      shared loader used by the GraphQL resolver)
    TestPersonaCLI_LoadUsesProjectOverride (precedence applied in the
      `persona load` injection path too)

AC#5 — UI explainer copy:
  cli/internal/server/frontend/src/routes/nodes/[nodeId]/projects/
    [projectId]/personas/PersonasView.svelte
  `data-testid="personas-explainer"` carries two sentences:
    - "Personas are AI personality templates..."
    - "Library personas are shared across projects and are read-only;
       project personas live with this project..."
  Playwright: personas.e2e.ts -> "personas page shows explainer..."

AC#6 — Source badge:
  Each persona row renders `data-testid="persona-source-<name>"` with
  `library` or `project` text. Library badges use zinc; project badges
  use teal so they read as the "active/editable" source.
  Playwright: personas.e2e.ts same test asserts both values.

AC#7 — New / Edit / Delete for project personas:
  - Header `New persona` button opens the editor dialog.
  - Project rows expose `persona-edit-<name>` and
    `persona-delete-<name>` buttons; library rows expose neither.
  - Editor dialog persists via personaCreate/personaUpdate.
  - Delete dialog confirms and calls personaDelete.
  Playwright: "create edit delete project persona" covers all three.

AC#8 — Fork library to project:
  Library rows expose `persona-fork-<name>`. On click:
    - If the library name already collides with a proe; consumers that don't know about it continue
to work.

./.ddx/executions/20260424T031408-2354ccd8/manifest.json:13:    "description": "## Observed / Motivation

`.ddx/executions/<ts>-<hash>/{manifest.json, prompt.md, result.json}` bundles are already being produced per `ddx agent execute-bead` attempt — 469 of them on the current repo. They contain the canonical record of what an agent was asked to do and what verdict the attempt produced. **Nothing in the web UI surfaces them today.** A user who wants to ask "why did this bead end up in this state?" has to drop to the filesystem.

Sessions (`sessions.jsonl` / sharded index per `ddx-2ceb02fa`) carry the harness/cost/token telemetry but not the verdict and not the bead linkage. Agent log streams (`agent-*.jsonl`) carry the tool-call trace but are scattered across hundreds of files. **Executions are the natural join** — they connect a bead, a session, a prompt, a verdict, and a tool-call stream.

## Ground truth

Per-execution on disk:

```
.ddx/executions/<ts>-<hash>/
  manifest.json      { harness, model, base_rev, result_rev, verdict, bead_id, execution_dir }
  prompt.md          the prompt that was sent
  result.json        { verdict, rationale, ... }
```

Relations:
- `manifest.bead_id` → bead
- a session id is recorded in bead events (`kind:cost`, `kind:routing`) and in the agent-log stream; both can map back to an execution via correlation metadata
- tool calls live in `.ddx/agent-logs/agent-*.jsonl` (and `agent-s-*.jsonl`), keyed by session id

## Scope

### Part 1 — GraphQL surface

- `executions(projectId, first, after, beadId?, since?, until?, verdict?, harness?)` — paginated list of execution bundles.
- `execution(id)` — one bundle, fully materialized: manifest fields, prompt markdown, result JSON, linked bead (id + title), linked session (id + harness + model + cost + token totals), tool-call stream pointer.
- `executionToolCalls(id, first, after)` — paginated tool-call stream for the execution, read lazily from the agent-log file. Matches the event shape used by the workers detail page.

Executions source of truth: directory scan of `.ddx/executions/` filtered/paginated at the resolver. Shard discipline: follow the sessions-index monthly shard pattern (`ddx-2ceb02fa`) if listing all executions grows beyond tens of thousands — per standing directive `ddx-9ce6842a`, raise the storage decision with the user before switching substrate.

### Part 2 — Sidebar + routes

- Add "Executions" to the project sidebar (`NavShell.svelte`).
- Routes:
  - `/nodes/<n>/projects/<p>/executions` — list view with filters (verdict, harness, bead, date range, free-text over prompt). Table columns: timestamp, bead, harness/model, verdict (pass/block/error), duration, cost.
  - `/nodes/<n>/projects/<p>/executions/<id>` — detail view with five tabs: **Manifest**, **Prompt** (rendered markdown), **Result** (rationale + raw JSON toggle), **Session** (harness, model, cost, token totals, link to `/sessions/<id>`), **Tool calls** (virtualized lazy-scroll list of tool_use + tool_result pairs with expandable payloads).

### Part 3 — Cross-links (make the graph real)

- **Bead detail:** new "Executions" section listing all executions for this bead with verdict + timestamp + link. Already have data: manifest.bead_id.
- **Session detail:** new "Execution" link pointing to the execution this session ran for, when the session has a linked execution.
- **Commits page:** already shows commits; if a commit was produced by an execution (result_rev == commit sha), surface the execution link.
- **Graph/Docs pages:** out of scope for this bead.

### Part 4 — Perf

- Fixture: ≥1,000 executions, per standing directive `ddx-9ce6842a`. Tool-call streams seeded for 10% of them.
- Targets: list view p95 ≤ 200ms HTTP, detail view without tool calls p95 ≤ 150ms HTTP, tool-call page-1 (first 50) p95 ≤ 300ms HTTP.
- If these can't be met with filesystem scan + in-memory filter, raise DB-substrate decision with user per standing directive.

## Out of scope

- Writing new execution bundles (that's already happening).
- Executions from other projects/nodes (keep per-project for v1).
- Tool-call payload redaction / privacy. File a follow-up if relevant.
- `.claude/worktrees/` worktree lifecycle tracking — covered by companion bead per user's split request.",

Add ddx agent metrics tier-success command that aggregates execution outcomes by harness/model tier.

## Context
Execution evidence result.json records harness, concrete model (via manifest.json or result.json), outcome (task_succeeded/task_failed/etc.), cost_usd, and duration_ms. This command aggregates those into tier-level success rates to inform routing policy.

## Files to touch
- cli/cmd/agent_metrics.go — new subcommand under ddx agent metrics
- cli/cmd/agent.go — register metrics subcommand group
- cli/cmd/agent_metrics_test.go — test with fixture execution dirs

## Implementation notes
- Scan .ddx/executions/*/result.json for harness and outcome fields
- Group by harness (or harness+model if useful) 
- Calculate success rate = count(task_succeeded) / count(all)
- Include avg cost and duration per tier",

./.ddx/executions/20260425T073541-4f46941d/prompt.md:11:## 2026-04-22 — User directives on fixture size and storage strategy

Two standing instructions from the user that apply to this bead and to all other perf beads (`ddx-ad0db8fd`, `ddx-2ceb02fa`, and any spawned from efficacy/providers work):

1. **Minimum fixture size: 1,000s of records.** The original acceptance criterion (10 projects × 500 beads = 5,000) already meets this for beads, but restate the floor explicitly: **no perf work lands against a fixture smaller than 1,000 records per query shape**. Sessions fixtures, efficacy fixtures, provider/quota history fixtures — all ≥1k rows. Beads fixture stays at 5k; sessions fixture for `ddx-2ceb02fa` should be ≥10k index entries (already specified); efficacy and any new surfaces ≥1k attempts/rows each.

2. **DB backend is on the table.** If in-place optimization (scope, pushdown, lightweight index) cannot hit the target latencies on the fixture sizes above, the user is open to introducing a real data store — candidates named: **axon**, **sqlite**, **postgres**. Do not silently pick one: **raise the decision with the user before choosing a path.** Capture the baseline, capture what in-place optimization achieved, then bring the choice (and the rationale) to the user.

These directives supersede any acceptance criterion that implies "optimize in place no matter what." If the numbers say storage needs to change, that's the outcome — but the user decides the substrate.

**AC §8 — Playwright cross-project test routes wrong path**: `beads-smoke.spec.ts:107` navigates to `/nodes/${NODE_INFO.id}/projects/${PROJECT_ID}/beads` for the "cross-project" test, which is the same per-project URL as the 1s test. A cross-project smoke test should hit the all-beads route (e.g., `/nodes/${NODE_INFO.id}/beads` or whatever the actual cross-project URL is) and mock the `beads` query, not `beadsByProject`. The mock for `beads` exists (lines 76-89) but is never exercised because both tests navigate to the project-scoped path.
**AC §9 — Bead notes cross-reference**: The acceptance criteria require this bead's notes to list `ddx-ad0db8fd` and `ddx-2ceb02fa`. The baseline `.md` and code comments mention them, but no `ddx bead update` call or notes update appears in the diff. If the notes were updated via a separate mechanism not captured in this branch, this finding can be dismissed.

## 2026-04-23 — Baseline and perf-thread cross-reference

Baseline recorded in `docs/helix/06-iterate/perf/2026-04-22-baseline.md` and JSON sibling from the 10 projects x 500 beads fixture (5,000 beads total, 1,000 session rows). Environment: `go=go1.26.2 goos=linux goarch=arm64 cpu-cores=12`.

Key p95 results: `bead` 6.72ms in-process / 6.80ms HTTP; `beadsByProject` 8.84ms in-process / 9.29ms HTTP; `beads_crossProject` 62.16ms in-process / 75.80ms HTTP; `beadsByProject_statusOpen` 6.45ms in-process / 6.87ms HTTP.

Cross-reference for inherited harness work: `ddx-ad0db8fd` covers single-bead detail lookup; `ddx-2ceb02fa` covers sessions shard/index work. Both should use/extend this GraphQL perf harness.

Review follow-up: AC §8 cross-project Playwright smoke now navigates to `/nodes/{nodeId}/beads` and exercises the `beads` GraphQL query instead of the project-scoped `beadsByProject` path. Proposed CI posture remains a follow-up +25% p95 regression gate, not implemented in this bead.

## 2026-04-23 — Baseline refresh after session-index fixture alignment

Re-ran `BENCH_REPORT_DATE=2026-04-22 make bench-graphql` after updating the perf fixture to seed current pointer-only session-index shards instead of legacy `sessions.jsonl`. Refreshed artifacts: `docs/helix/06-iterate/perf/2026-04-22-baseline.md` and `.json`.

Refreshed p95 results: `bead` 7.23ms in-process / 7.29ms HTTP; `beadsByProject` 7.18ms in-process / 7.83ms HTTP; `beads_crossProject` 62.49ms in-process / 67.29ms HTTP; `beadsByProject_statusOpen` 6.74ms in-process / 7.73ms HTTP; `agentSessions` 13.45ms in-process / 14.19ms HTTP.

## 2026-04-23 — Final `make bench-graphql` verification

After fixing `cli/Makefile` to pass an absolute `DDX_BENCH_REPORT_DIR`, `BENCH_REPORT_DATE=2026-04-22 make bench-graphql` writes to the intended repo docs path. Final p95 results from that run: `bead` 6.76ms in-process / 7.20ms HTTP; `beadsByProject` 6.88ms in-process / 7.85ms HTTP; `beads_crossProject` 63.22ms in-process / 71.82ms HTTP; `beadsByProject_statusOpen` 6.75ms in-process / 7.56ms HTTP; `agentSessions` 13.24ms in-process / 13.64ms HTTP.

./.ddx/executions/20260416T202842-3d9d211b/prompt.md:5:## Scope
Add ddx agent metrics tier-success command that aggregates execution outcomes by harness/model tier.

## Context
Execution evidence result.json records harness, concrete model (via manifest.json or result.json), outcome (task_succeeded/task_failed/etc.), cost_usd, and duration_ms. This command aggregates those into tier-level success rates to inform routing policy.

## Files to touch
- cli/cmd/agent_metrics.go — new subcommand under ddx agent metrics
- cli/cmd/agent.go — register metrics subcommand group
- cli/cmd/agent_metrics_test.go — test with fixture execution dirs

## Implementation notes
- Scan .ddx/executions/*/result.json for harness and outcome fields
- Group by harness (or harness+model if useful) 
- Calculate success rate = count(task_succeeded) / count(all)
- Include avg cost and duration per tier
